Who we are

A tertiary-care maternal-fetal medicine service.

Compass is a clinical decision-support tool for maternal-fetal medicine, our maternal-fetal medicine and prenatal diagnosis program provides care for complex pregnancies across south-central Ontario.

This site is built for our team — staff, fellows, residents, sonographers, nurses, and allied health colleagues. It is intended to put validated calculators, reference ranges, and decision-support material within reach during clinical work, without distraction.

Read more →
01

Advanced fetal assessment

Ultrasound, Doppler, cardiac assessment, and prenatal diagnostic procedures.

02

Multidisciplinary consultation

Genetics, neonatology, paediatric subspecialties, anaesthesia, and obstetric medicine.

03

Education & training

Resident, fellow, and allied health teaching across the perinatal continuum.

04

Research & quality improvement

Clinical research, audit, and translation of evidence into local practice.

Home › About

About Compass

A clinical decision-support hub for maternal-fetal medicine.

Compass is a focused, mobile-friendly digital tool built for the day-to-day clinical work of our team. The intent is simple: when a calculator, reference range, or protocol is needed at the bedside, in clinic, on call, or during ultrasound review, it should be one or two taps away — not buried in a textbook, a paper chart, or a slow-loading hospital intranet page.

Scope

The platform covers fetal biometry and growth, pregnancy dating, fetal Doppler assessment, cervical length, amniotic fluid, and a growing library of related decision-support tools. New calculators are added as their underlying reference data have been reviewed against the source publication and approved by the clinical owner.

Governance

Every calculator displays the formula or reference standard used, the original source, the version number, and the date of last review. When a reference is updated or replaced, the calculator version is incremented and the change is noted. Calculators with reference data still pending institutional review are marked clearly and remain inactive until validated.

Privacy

Compass does not collect, store, or transmit patient identifiers. All calculations run locally in the user's browser. There are no analytics, no cookies for tracking, and no server-side logging of inputs. The tool is designed to be safe to open from a personal phone without entering identifiable information.

How to contribute

If you spot an error, a stale reference, or a calculator that would help the service, please contact the clinical owner. Reference data must be accompanied by the source publication so that values can be verified against the original paper before they are added.

Disclaimer

Compass is intended as clinical decision support and education for qualified clinicians. It does not replace clinical judgement, local protocols, or consultation with the appropriate specialist. Results must be interpreted in the full clinical context. The tool has not been certified as a medical device. Users should verify the cited source and confirm applicability before acting on any output.

Home › Calculators

Clinical calculators

Searchable library of fetal assessment and pregnancy-care calculators. Filter by category, or search for a specific tool.

Within CNS:
Calculators › Hadlock EFW

Hadlock estimated fetal weight

Estimated fetal weight (Hadlock-4) and percentile against the Hadlock 1991 in-utero growth standard.

Ready Hadlock FP et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1985 (formula) · Hadlock FP et al. Radiology 1991 (percentiles) v0.1 · Singleton
For clinical decision support only. Interpret in full clinical context. Reference standard pending institutional validation against the source PDF.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
mm
mm
mm
Result

Enter biometry above and tap Calculate to estimate fetal weight and percentile.

EFW vs gestational age
Hadlock 1991 in-utero growth standard · 5 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 95 percentiles
50th centile
10th / 90th
5th / 95th
This measurement
How to interpret this result

The estimated fetal weight is calculated using Hadlock formula 4 (BPD, HC, AC, FL) and compared to the Hadlock 1991 in-utero growth standard for singletons.

  • An EFW below the 10th percentile defines small-for-gestational-age (SGA) and warrants further assessment for fetal growth restriction (FGR).
  • An EFW below the 3rd percentile, or with abnormal Dopplers, supports a diagnosis of FGR per ISUOG / SMFM consensus.
  • An EFW above the 90th percentile is large-for-gestational-age (LGA); above the 95th, consider macrosomia risk in context of maternal diabetes and gestational age.
  • Always interpret EFW alongside interval growth, Dopplers, amniotic fluid, and the clinical picture.
Limitations

EFW carries an inherent measurement error of approximately ±10–15% even in optimal conditions. Limitations to consider:

  • Hadlock 1991 was derived from a predominantly Caucasian US population — a customised or population-specific standard (e.g. INTERGROWTH-21st, WHO, GROW) may give different percentiles.
  • The formula assumes accurate dating; use the earliest reliable scan or LMP-confirmed CRL dating.
  • Calculator does not adjust for ethnicity, parity, maternal weight, or fetal sex.
  • Limited reliability at extremes of gestational age and weight.
  • For multiple pregnancy, use a twin-specific reference (not yet available in Compass).
Reference population

Hadlock FP, Harrist RB, Martinez-Poyer J. In utero analysis of fetal growth: a sonographic weight standard. Radiology 1991;181(1):129–133.

The 1991 standard is based on serial ultrasound assessment of 392 normal singleton pregnancies in Houston, Texas, between 10 and 41 weeks of gestation.

The Hadlock-4 formula (BPD + HC + AC + FL) is from Hadlock FP et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1985;151(3):333–337.

Audit & reference
Formula
Hadlock-4 (BPD, HC, AC, FL)
Percentile source
Hadlock 1991 in-utero growth curve
GA range
14+0 – 40+6 weeks
Population
Singleton, mixed sex
Version
v0.1 (pending PDF validation)
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Reviewer
Pending
Calculators › CRL dating

Pregnancy dating by crown-rump length

First-trimester gestational age estimation from crown-rump length, compared across three published reference standards. The selected primary reference drives the headline value; all three are computed side-by-side for cross-checking.

Ready Robinson & Fleming 1975 · INTERGROWTH-21st 2014 · Daya 1993 v0.3 · Singleton & twin
Most accurate between CRL 10–84 mm. Daya 1993 (an IVF-anchored equation derived locally at McMaster) extends the lower bound to 2 mm. For CRL > 84 mm (≈14 weeks), use BPD or HC-based dating.
Inputs

Spontaneously conceived twins: date from the larger of the two CRLs (SOGC No. 428 & 440; ISUOG 2016). IVF / ART: date from the conception date — oocyte-retrieval or embryo-transfer date — not from CRL biometry.

mm
mm
mm
Result

Enter the crown-rump length and tap Calculate to derive gestational age and EDD.

How to interpret this result

CRL is the most accurate biometric marker for pregnancy dating between approximately 7 and 14 weeks. The estimated gestational age and corresponding EDD assume a single, well-visualised fetal pole measured in a neutral fetal position with calipers placed at the outermost cranial and caudal landmarks.

  • Robinson & Fleming 1975 remains the reference standard in many centres; widely used and well-validated. Derived from menstrual dates.
  • INTERGROWTH-21st 2014 is a contemporary, internationally derived standard that gives broadly similar results, with minor differences at the extremes.
  • Daya 1993 (Salim Daya, McMaster University, Hamilton) is anchored to true conception date in 94 IVF singleton pregnancies, where day of oocyte retrieval = day 14. This makes it one of the few CRL-dating equations free of LMP / ovulation-day uncertainty; outside the 40–60 mm range, it tends to estimate slightly earlier GAs than equations based on menstrual dates.
  • If CRL-based GA differs from LMP-based GA by more than the accepted threshold (commonly ≥5–7 days in the first trimester), the scan-based GA is generally preferred.
Limitations

Accuracy depends on technique. Common sources of error:

  • Flexed or hyperextended fetal position causing under- or over-estimation.
  • Caliper placement on yolk sac or amnion rather than crown / rump.
  • CRL > 84 mm: fetal flexion limits accuracy — use HC or BPD instead.
  • CRL < 10 mm: small absolute errors translate to large relative errors in GA.
  • In twin pregnancies, switch to Twin mode above: spontaneously conceived twins are dated from the larger CRL, and IVF / ART pregnancies should be dated from the conception date rather than biometry.
Dating twin pregnancies

In Twin mode the calculator dates the pregnancy from the larger of the two crown-rump lengths and reports the inter-twin CRL discordance.

  • Spontaneously conceived twins: SOGC (No. 428 dichorionic, No. 440 monochorionic) and ISUOG (2016) recommend using the larger CRL to estimate gestational age, erring toward overestimation so as not to miss early growth restriction in the smaller twin.
  • IVF / ART pregnancies: date from the known conception date — day of oocyte retrieval or embryo transfer — which is more accurate than any biometric equation. CRL dating is not required.
  • CRL discordance: calculated as (larger − smaller) ÷ larger × 100. The median CRL discordance in twins is ≈ 3.5–3.8%, with a 95th centile near 14.3% (SOGC No. 428). ISUOG advises that a CRL discordance ≥ 10% — and the SOGC notes that discordance ≥ 10%, and particularly ≥ 15–20%, is associated with aneuploidy, structural anomaly, early fetal loss, and (in monochorionic twins) subsequent TTTS — should prompt discussion with a fetal medicine specialist and detailed assessment.
  • If chorionicity cannot be confidently established, the pregnancy should be monitored as if monochorionic.

Sources: SOGC Guideline No. 428 (DC twins) JOGC 2022;44:819–834 · SOGC Guideline No. 440 (MC twins) JOGC 2023;45:587–606 · Khalil A et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: role of ultrasound in twin pregnancy. UOG 2016;47:247–263.

Audit & reference
Formulas
Robinson-Fleming: GA(d) = 8.052 × √(CRL × 1.037) + 23.73
INTERGROWTH-21st: GA(d) = 40.9041 + 3.21585 × √CRL + 0.348956 × CRL
Daya: GA(d) = 40.447 + 1.125 × CRL − 0.0058 × CRL²
Sources
Robinson HP, Fleming JE. Br J Obstet Gynaecol 1975;82(9):702–10.
Papageorghiou AT, et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014;44(6):641–8.
Daya S. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1993;168(3):903–8. [Local: McMaster University, Chedoke-McMaster Hospitals, Hamilton; n=94 IVF singletons]
Valid range
CRL 10–84 mm (Robinson-Fleming, INTERGROWTH-21st); CRL 2–80 mm (Daya)
Version
v0.3 (singleton & twin modes)
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Calculators › GA / date converter

GA / date converter

Enter any two of EDD, Date, or Gestational age — the third fills in automatically. Useful for back-dating prior reports, projecting forward, or deriving the EDD from a known GA at a known date.

Ready 3-way bidirectional · 280-day Naegele convention v0.6
Conversion uses the 40-week (280-day) term convention. EDD set by ultrasound dating may differ from LMP-based EDD; always anchor against the clinically chosen EDD.
EDD
Date & gestational age
⇄
wk
d

Tip: pick a Date and a GA — the EDD above auto-fills. Or set the EDD first and Date ↔ GA convert against it.

Summary

Enter an EDD, then a date or GA.

Calculators › UA-PI percentile

Umbilical artery pulsatility index

UA-PI percentile against the Acharya 2005 longitudinal reference for serial measurements at the free loop of cord.

Ready Acharya G et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2005;192:937–944 v0.2 · Singleton · 19–41 wk
Measure at the free loop of cord during fetal quiescence. Insonation angle ≤15°. Result represents placental impedance.
Inputs
wk
d
PI
Result

Enter GA and UA-PI to compute percentile and Z-score.

UA-PI vs gestational age
Acharya 2005 longitudinal reference · 5 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 95 percentiles
50th centile
10th / 90th
5th / 95th
This measurement
How to interpret this result

UA-PI reflects impedance to flow in the placental circulation. PI decreases with advancing gestation as placental vascular resistance falls.

  • UA-PI > 95th percentile indicates increased placental resistance and is consistent with placental insufficiency. Combined with EFW < 10th percentile, supports diagnosis of fetal growth restriction.
  • Absent end-diastolic flow (AEDV) is more severe and warrants intensive surveillance.
  • Reversed end-diastolic flow (REDV) indicates critical placental compromise — typically prompts delivery consideration after corticosteroids if >26 weeks.
  • Always interpret alongside MCA-PI, CPR, AFI, and biophysical profile.
Limitations
  • Reference range is for the free loop of cord. PI differs at the fetal and placental ends — Acharya reports the free loop as standard.
  • Singleton pregnancies only. Twin reference ranges differ and are not implemented in Compass.
  • Intra-observer coefficient of variation reported as 10.5%. Take 3 sets of waveforms and use the average.
  • Insonation angle >60° introduces error — keep angle ≤15° per Acharya methodology.
  • Maternal smoking, fetal arrhythmia, and uterine contractions can transiently elevate PI.
Reference population

Acharya G, Wilsgaard T, Berntsen GKR, Maltau JM, Kiserud T. Reference ranges for serial measurements of umbilical artery Doppler indices in the second half of pregnancy. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2005;192:937–944.

Longitudinal study of 130 low-risk singleton pregnancies in Tromsø, Norway, with 513 paired observations between 19 and 42 weeks at 4-week intervals. Multilevel modelling with fractional polynomial fits. Free-loop measurements with angle ≤15°.

Audit & reference
Formula
μ = 1.5075 − 0.2843 × √GA
σ² = 0.0667 + 0.00398·GA − 0.0276 × √GA
Z = ln(PI), centiles = exp(μ ± z·σ)
GA range
19+0 – 41+0 weeks
Population
Singleton, low-risk, Norwegian
Version
v0.2
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Reviewer
Pending
Calculators › MCA-PI percentile

Middle cerebral artery pulsatility index

MCA-PI percentile against the Ebbing 2007 longitudinal reference. Reduced MCA-PI suggests cerebral redistribution.

Ready Ebbing C et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:287–296 v0.2 · Singleton · 21–39 wk
Sample at the proximal third of the MCA (origin from circle of Willis). Avoid excessive transducer pressure on the fetal head — this falsely elevates PI.
Inputs
wk
d
PI
Result

Enter GA and MCA-PI to compute percentile and Z-score.

MCA-PI vs gestational age
Ebbing 2007 longitudinal reference · 5 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 95 percentiles
50th centile
10th / 90th
5th / 95th
This measurement
How to interpret this result

MCA-PI reflects cerebral vascular impedance. The reference curve has an inverted U-shape, peaking around 28–30 weeks then declining toward term as cerebral resistance physiologically falls.

  • MCA-PI < 5th percentile indicates cerebral redistribution (the "brain-sparing" effect) and suggests the fetus is autoregulating in response to hypoxia.
  • Cerebral redistribution in a small fetus is a defining feature of fetal growth restriction per ISUOG/SMFM consensus.
  • An isolated low MCA-PI in an appropriately grown fetus near term may reflect placental insufficiency without overt growth restriction — interpret with EFW, UA-PI, and CPR.
  • MCA-PI rises again as anemia progresses; high MCA-PI does not exclude anemia — use MCA-PSV instead.
Limitations
  • Sample at the proximal third of the vessel; PI is significantly higher in the middle and distal thirds.
  • Excessive transducer pressure compresses the fetal skull and falsely elevates PI — use a light touch.
  • Reference range is 21–39 weeks; values outside this range are extrapolated and less reliable.
  • Singleton only.
  • Behavioural state affects MCA Doppler — record during quiet (non-REM) periods.
Reference population

Ebbing C, Rasmussen S, Kiserud T. Middle cerebral artery blood flow velocities and pulsatility index and the cerebroplacental pulsatility ratio: longitudinal reference ranges and terms for serial measurements. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:287–296.

Longitudinal study of 161 low-risk singleton pregnancies in Bergen, Norway, with 566 MCA observations between 19 and 41 weeks at 3–5 week intervals. Same statistical framework as Acharya 2005.

Audit & reference
Reference
Ebbing 2007, Table 3 (P5/P10/P50/P90/P95)
Method
Log-normal interpolation between weekly values
GA range
21+0 – 39+0 weeks
Population
Singleton, low-risk, Norwegian
Version
v0.2
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Calculators › Cerebroplacental ratio

Cerebroplacental ratio (CPR)

CPR percentile (MCA-PI ÷ UA-PI) against the Ebbing 2007 paired longitudinal reference. Sensitive marker of placental compromise.

Ready Ebbing C et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:287–296 v0.2 · Singleton · 21–39 wk
Reference is built from paired MCA-PI and UA-PI measurements in the same fetuses — this is more accurate than dividing two separately-derived percentile tables.
Inputs
wk
d
PI
PI
Result

Enter GA, MCA-PI, and UA-PI to compute CPR and percentile.

CPR vs gestational age
Ebbing 2007 longitudinal reference · 5 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 95 percentiles
50th centile
10th / 90th
5th / 95th
This measurement
How to interpret this result

CPR captures simultaneous cerebral redistribution and rising placental resistance. It is more sensitive than UA-PI or MCA-PI alone for predicting adverse perinatal outcome.

  • CPR < 5th percentile indicates redistributed circulation — concerning even when UA-PI and MCA-PI are individually within reference range.
  • An absolute cutoff of CPR < 1.0 is widely used and considered clinically actionable; many guidelines combine percentile and absolute cutoffs.
  • Low CPR at term in an apparently appropriately grown fetus is associated with failure to reach growth potential, increased Cesarean for fetal distress, and adverse neonatal outcome.
  • CPR is a defining feature of late-onset FGR per Delphi consensus.
Limitations
  • Both PI values must be measured with proper technique (free loop for UA, proximal MCA, low transducer pressure).
  • CPR has the limitations of both component measures combined.
  • Reference range 21–39 weeks; less validated outside this window.
  • Singleton only.
  • Threshold for clinical action varies by guideline — local protocols apply.
Reference population

Ebbing C, Rasmussen S, Kiserud T. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:287–296. CPR reference is based on 550 paired MCA/UA observations in 161 low-risk singleton pregnancies. Built from paired data — methodologically superior to dividing separately-derived percentile tables.

Note: alternative cross-sectional CPR references (Baschat 2003, Arduini 1990) give slightly different percentile values. Ebbing is preferred for serial monitoring.

Audit & reference
Reference
Ebbing 2007, Table 5 (paired CPR percentiles)
Method
Log-normal interpolation between weekly values
Calculation
CPR = MCA-PI ÷ UA-PI
GA range
21+0 – 39+0 weeks
Version
v0.2
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Calculators › MCA-PSV MoM

MCA peak systolic velocity — MoM

MCA-PSV in multiples of the median for fetal anemia screening. The 1.5 MoM threshold has 100% sensitivity for moderate-severe anemia.

Ready Mari G et al. N Engl J Med 2000;342:9–14 v0.2 · Singleton · 18–40 wk
Sample close to MCA origin from internal carotid. Insonation angle close to 0°. Use the highest of three consistent waveforms during fetal quiescence.
Inputs
wk
d
cm/s
Result

Enter GA and MCA-PSV to compute MoM and anemia risk category.

MCA-PSV vs gestational age
Mari 2000 reference · median and 1.5 MoM threshold
Median (1.0 MoM)
1.5 MoM threshold
This measurement
How to interpret this result

MCA-PSV rises in fetal anemia due to increased cardiac output and reduced blood viscosity. MoM thresholds (Mari 2000):

  • < 1.29 MoM — no significant anemia. Continue routine surveillance.
  • 1.29–1.49 MoM — borderline / suspicious. Repeat in 1–2 days.
  • ≥ 1.50 MoM — moderate-to-severe anemia. 100% sensitivity with 12% false positive rate. Proceed to fetal blood sampling and intrauterine transfusion as indicated.
  • ≥ 1.55 MoM — severe anemia.

Hydrops on ultrasound implies severe anemia regardless of MoM and warrants urgent intervention.

Limitations
  • Validated for red-cell alloimmunization. Performance in parvovirus B19, fetomaternal haemorrhage, and other anemia causes is similar but less rigorously studied.
  • Loses sensitivity after 35 weeks — false-positive rate rises. Use with caution near term.
  • Sensitivity drops after a previous intrauterine transfusion (the post-transfusion red cells alter rheology). Higher MoM thresholds (e.g. 1.69) have been proposed in this setting.
  • Technique-sensitive: angle of insonation, sampling location along the MCA, and behavioural state affect results.
  • Singleton only.
Reference population

Mari G, for the Collaborative Group for Doppler Assessment of the Blood Velocity in Anemic Fetuses. Noninvasive diagnosis by Doppler ultrasonography of fetal anemia due to maternal red-cell alloimmunization. N Engl J Med 2000;342:9–14.

Multicentre prospective study of 111 fetuses at risk for anemia compared against 265 normal fetuses. Established MoM thresholds with 100% sensitivity for moderate-severe anemia.

Audit & reference
Formula
Median MCA-PSV (cm/s) = e(2.31 + 0.046 × GA)
MoM = measured value ÷ median
Thresholds
1.29 / 1.50 / 1.55 MoM (mild / moderate / severe)
GA range
18+0 – 40+0 weeks
Population
Singleton, alloimmunized pregnancies
Version
v0.2
Last reviewed
Pending institutional review
Calculators › Nasal bone

Nasal bone length & hypoplasia

Multi-nomogram nasal bone assessment with Down syndrome screening utility. Hypoplasia defined as nasal bone length below the 2.5th percentile for gestational age.

Ready Sonek 2003 · Odibo 2007 · Bunduki 2003 · Cicero 2003 · Bromley 2002 v0.5 · 11–40 wk
Strict mid-sagittal view at 45° insonation. Measure the bony segment only, caliper-to-caliper. Hypoplasia by percentile uses Sonek/Bunduki; Odibo retains its published MoM < 0.75 rule and Cicero retains < 2.5 mm for the trisomy 21 likelihood ratio.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and nasal bone length. Results update as you type.

Nasal bone length vs gestational age
Sonek 2003 reference (n = 3537) · 2.5 / 50 / 97.5 percentiles
50th centile
2.5th / 97.5th
This measurement
How to measure the nasal bone

Plane. Strict mid-sagittal view of the fetal face showing nasal bone, lips, maxilla, and mandible in continuity.

Insonation angle. 45° or 135° to the long axis of the nose. Angles approaching 90° make the upper nasal bone edge difficult to delineate; very acute angles tend to underestimate length.

Calipers. Place at the proximal end (frontonasal synostosis) and the distal tip of the ossified bony segment. Measure bone only — not overlying skin.

Pitfalls. An occiput-anterior head position obscures the profile — wait or rescan. Three independent measurements averaged is reasonable practice.

Teaching image

No image uploaded yet.

About the references
  1. Sonek JD et al. Nasal bone length throughout gestation: normal ranges based on 3537 fetal ultrasound measurements. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2003;21:152–155. [Primary · 11–40 wk]
  2. Odibo AO et al. Defining nasal bone hypoplasia in second-trimester Down syndrome screening: does the use of multiples of the median improve screening efficacy? Am J Obstet Gynecol 2007;197:361.e1–4.
  3. Bunduki V et al. Fetal nasal bone length: reference range and clinical application in ultrasound screening for trisomy 21. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2003;21:156–160.
  4. Cicero S et al. Nasal bone hypoplasia in trisomy 21 at 15–22 weeks' gestation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2003;21:15–18.
  5. Bromley B et al. Fetal nose bone length: a marker for Down syndrome in the second trimester. J Ultrasound Med 2002;21:1387–1394.
Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Cavum septi pellucidi

Cavum septi pellucidi (CSP) width

Multi-nomogram CSP width assessment. Wide CSP (> P95) raises consideration of 22q11.2 deletion and aneuploidy; narrow or absent CSP (< P5) suggests partial agenesis of the corpus callosum, holoprosencephaly, or septo-optic dysplasia. Default reference is Sichitiu 2024.

Ready Sichitiu 2024 · Falco 2000 · Arisoy 2022 v0.7 · 16–36 wk (Sichitiu)
Measure on the axial transventricular plane, calipers on the inner aspect of the lateral CSP borders (inner-to-inner). The CSP should be a well-delineated box-like hypoechoic structure. Failure to visualise between 18–37 wk warrants targeted neurosonography. Isolated wide or narrow CSP on otherwise normal neurosonography appears not to increase short-term neurodevelopmental risk (Sichitiu 2024), but karyotype/microarray for 22q11.2 should still be considered for a wide CSP.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and CSP width. Results update as you type.

CSP width vs gestational age
Sichitiu 2024 (LMS) · 16–36 wk · P5 / P50 / P95
P50 (median)
P5 / P95
This measurement
How to measure

Plane. Axial transventricular plane of the fetal head. The CSP appears as a well-delineated rectangular hypoechoic box anterior to the thalami, between the frontal horns of the lateral ventricles.

Calipers. Place inner-to-inner on the lateral borders of the CSP at its widest point, perpendicular to the falx cerebri (Sichitiu, Falco, Arisoy all use this convention).

Visibility window. The CSP should be visible between 18 and 37 weeks (100% in Falco 2000 within this window). Non-visualisation in this window mandates targeted neurosonography to rule out agenesis of the corpus callosum, holoprosencephaly, septo-optic dysplasia, schizencephaly, and severe hydrocephalus.

Pitfalls. The columns of the fornix posterior to the CSP can be mistaken for a separate cavity (Callen 2008). The cavum vergae (CV) — a posterior extension of the CSP between corpus callosum and fornix — is a normal variant in up to 7.8% of fetuses 25–39 wk (Tao 2013) and should not be reported as abnormal when present in isolation.

Clinical thresholds. < P5 = narrow; > P95 = wide. Wide CSP carries a reported association with 22q11.2 microdeletion (Chaoui 2016 — 67.5% of confirmed 22q11 cases had > P95 CSP) and trisomies 21/13/18, but in low-risk neurosonographically-normal fetuses the developmental impact appears minimal (Sichitiu 2024).

About the references
  1. Sichitiu J, Ghannad-Zadeh K, Van Mieghem T, et al. Revisiting the implications of a wide or narrow fetal cavum septi pellucidi. J Ultrasound Med 2024;43:1461–1466. [Primary · LMS method · 138 low-risk Toronto fetuses · 16–36 wk]
  2. Falco P, Gabrielli S, Visentin A, Perolo A, Pilu G, Bovicelli L. Transabdominal sonography of the cavum septum pellucidum in normal fetuses in the second and third trimesters of pregnancy. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2000;16:549–553. [Polynomial · 286 fetuses · 15–41 wk]
  3. Arisoy R, Karatas S, Semiz A, Sanlıkan F, Yayla M. Cavum septum pellucidum nomogram during the second trimester of pregnancy. J Obstet Gynaecol 2022;42:2931–2934. [Tabulated percentiles · 6042 fetuses · 15–28 wk]
  4. Tao G, Lu G, Zhan X, et al. Sonographic appearance of the cavum septum pellucidum et vergae in normal fetuses in the second and third trimesters of pregnancy. J Clin Ultrasound 2013;41:525–531. [Reference for cavum vergae morphology — descriptive, not used as a percentile model]
  5. Chaoui R, Heling KS, Zhao Y, Sinkovskaya E, Abuhamad A, Karl K. Dilated cavum septi pellucidi in fetuses with microdeletion 22q11. Prenat Diagn 2016;36:911–915. [22q11.2 association]
Calculators › Fetal systems › Chest › Lung-to-head ratio

Lung-to-head ratio (LHR) & o/e LHR

Sonographic severity stratification for the fetus with congenital diaphragmatic hernia. Observed LHR is computed by your selected technique; expected LHR uses the side-specific Peralta 2005 quadratic equations; the o/e LHR (%) is independent of gestational age and drives the standard Jani / Deprest severity bands used in TOTAL/FETO trial recruitment.

Ready Peralta. UOG. 2005 (reference) · Jani. UOG. 2007 (o/e LHR) · Jani. UOG. 2012 (technique) v0.9 · 12–32 wk (Peralta)
Measure the contralateral lung on the axial four-chamber view of the heart. For a left CDH measure the right lung; for a right CDH measure the left lung. Tracing is the most reproducible technique and is the method on which Peralta's published reference is based; the longest-axis and AP methods systematically overestimate the lung area, so the o/e value computed from a non-tracing observed LHR is inflated relative to the published severity bands. The published reference range is 12–32 weeks; o/e LHR is GA-independent in this window (Jani 2007).
Inputs
wk
d
Manual tracing of the lung perimeter on the four-chamber-view plane (most reproducible; Peralta 2005 reference is built from this technique).
mm²
mm
mm
mm
mm
mm
Assessment

Choose side, method, and enter the contralateral lung measurement plus head circumference. Results update as you type.

LHR vs gestational age
Peralta 2005 · 12–32 wk · P5 / P50 / P95
P50 (median, normal fetuses)
P5 / P95
This measurement
How to measure

Plane. Axial cross-sectional plane of the fetal thorax used for the four-chamber view of the heart. The fetus should be still and ideally facing the transducer for clearest lung borders.

Side and lung. The contralateral lung is measured. Left CDH (LCDH) → measure the right lung. Right CDH (RCDH) → measure the left lung.

Methods (Peralta 2005, Jani 2012). Three techniques are validated:

  • Tracing — manually trace the lung perimeter. Most reproducible and the technique on which the Peralta reference is built. Recommended.
  • Longest axis — multiply the longest diameter of the lung by its longest perpendicular diameter. Overestimates true (traced) area by ~48% (left lung) and ~43% (right lung) — applying these values to the tracing-based reference inflates the o/e LHR.
  • Anterior–posterior — multiply the AP diameter at the mid-clavicular line by the perpendicular diameter at its midpoint. Essentially unbiased on the left lung but overestimates the right lung area by ~36%.

Head circumference. Standard transthalamic biparietal-diameter view, ellipse around the outer skull edge, in millimetres.

Severity bands (Jani 2007 / Deprest). Calculated o/e LHR (%) — independent of gestational age in this window:

  • < 15% — extreme (typically lethal)
  • 15–25% — severe (FETO-eligible in TOTAL trial criteria)
  • 26–35% — moderate
  • 36–45% — mild
  • > 45% — favourable

Caveats. Right-sided CDH is associated with worse outcomes than left-sided CDH at any given o/e LHR; intrathoracic liver herniation and stomach position are independent prognostic factors that the LHR/o/e LHR alone does not capture. MRI-based total fetal lung volume is a complementary measure with similar predictive value.

Equations & reference

Observed LHR (mm). All linear measurements in mm, area in mm².

  • Tracing: LHR = lung area / HC
  • Longest axis: LHR = (DA × DB) / HC
  • Anterior–posterior: LHR = (DAP × D⊥) / HC

Expected LHR (Peralta 2005, manual tracing reference). GA in decimal weeks (weeks + days/7):

  • Expected left LHR = −1.4815 + 0.1824·GA − 0.0023·GA²
  • Expected right LHR = −2.2481 + 0.2712·GA − 0.0033·GA²

SD of LHR (Peralta 2005) — used for the P5/P95 reference band on the chart:

  • SDleft LHR = −0.0826 + 0.0139·GA
  • SDright LHR = −0.0509 + 0.0178·GA

o/e LHR (%) = (observed LHR / expected LHR) × 100.

References.

  1. Peralta CFA, Cavoretto P, Csapo B, Vandecruys H, Nicolaides KH. Assessment of lung area in normal fetuses at 12–32 weeks. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2005;26:718–724. [Reference equations · 650 normal fetuses · 12–32 wk]
  2. Jani J, Nicolaides KH, Keller RL, et al. Observed to expected lung area to head circumference ratio in the prediction of survival in fetuses with isolated diaphragmatic hernia. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:67–71. [o/e LHR concept · severity stratification]
  3. Jani JC, Peralta CFA, Nicolaides KH. Lung-to-head ratio: a need to unify the technique. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2012;39:2–6. [Tracing as preferred technique]
  4. Jani J, Peralta CFA, Benachi A, Deprest J, Nicolaides KH. Assessment of lung area in fetuses with congenital diaphragmatic hernia. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;30:72–76. [Inter- and intraobserver agreement of the three techniques]
  5. Deprest JA, Nicolaides KH, Benachi A, et al. Randomized trial of fetal surgery for severe left diaphragmatic hernia. N Engl J Med 2021;385:107–118. [TOTAL trial — severe LCDH definition]
Calculators › Fetal systems › Abdomen › Small bowel

Fetal small bowel diameter

Z-score and percentile of the fetal small bowel short-axis lumen diameter, scored in parallel against the Lap 2020 longitudinal reference (primary) and the Parulekar 1991 categorical thresholds.

Ready Lap. JMFNM. 2020 · Parulekar. JUM. 1991 v0.12 · 20–40 wk (Lap)
Lap 2020's central message: trajectory matters more than a single value. Progressive z-score > 8 after 25 wk was 100% predictive of postnatal small-bowel pathology; transient dilatation that resolved on serial scans had a normal outcome.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and the short-axis lumen diameter of the largest small-bowel loop. The z-score, percentile, and severity update as you type.

Small bowel diameter vs GA
Lap 2020 · 20–40 wk · mean and 95% prediction interval
Mean (Lap 2020)
95% prediction interval
This measurement
Tips & tricks for measurement

Plane. Coronal plane of the fetal abdomen, transducer angled to display the longest loops of bowel in cross-section. Avoid foreshortened oblique cuts.

What to look for. Small bowel sits centrally in the abdomen with closely apposed loops. After ~27 wk, peristalsis becomes visible (Parulekar saw small-bowel peristalsis in 9/243 fetuses 25–30 wk, rising to 77% at term). Valvulae conniventes are rarely seen on US (9/243 in Parulekar's series).

Caliper placement. Short-axis diameter, inner-to-inner bowel wall. Pick the largest loop on the displayed image.

Distinguish from colon. Small bowel = central, no haustra, peristaltic; colon = peripheral C-frame, haustra after 3rd trimester, hypoechoic meconium.

Common pitfalls.

  • Hyperechoic appearance of small-bowel region before 20 wk is a normal finding (Fakhry 1986, Lince 1985) — collapsed loops + mesenteric fat. Don't mistake for an abdominal mass.
  • Meconium ileus can mimic the normal hyperechoic pattern in early gestation. Look for proximal dilatation and absence of fluid-filled peristaltic loops to differentiate.
  • A single dilated loop is rarely diagnostic. Repeat in 2 wk — Lap 2020 found 56% of suspected dilatations resolved on serial scans.

When to escalate. Persistent z > 4 after 2 weeks → serial monitoring + pediatric surgery consult. Persistent z > 8 after 25 wk = pathology in 100% of Lap's cohort.

Equations & severity bands

Lap 2020 (primary). Linear model in GA. Mean and 95% prediction interval at 2-week increments interpolated linearly between published values. SD derived as (P97.5 − P2.5) / 3.92.

Z-score severity bands (derived from Lap's prognostic data):

  • z ≤ 2 — within reference
  • 2 < z ≤ 4 — mildly enlarged · repeat in 2–4 wk
  • 4 < z ≤ 8 — moderately enlarged · serial monitoring; pediatric surgery consult
  • z > 8 — pathological range (after 25 wk: 100% pathology in Lap series)

Parulekar 1991 (cross-check). GA-bucket categorical thresholds: small bowel "average" diameter and "largest observed" per 5-week bucket. The classical "small bowel should not exceed 6 mm at term" rule comes from this paper.

References
  1. Lap CCMM, Voskuilen CS, Pistorius LR, Mulder EJH, Visser GHA, Manten GTR. Reference curves for the normal fetal small bowel and colon diameters. J Matern Fetal Neonatal Med 2020;33(4):633–638.
  2. Parulekar SG. Sonography of normal fetal bowel. J Ultrasound Med 1991;10(4):211–220.
Calculators › Fetal systems › Abdomen › Large bowel

Fetal large bowel (colon) diameter

Z-score and percentile of the fetal colon lumen diameter scored in parallel against three published references: Lap 2020 (longitudinal), Zalel 2003 (descending colon, parasagittal), and Parulekar 1991 (categorical thresholds).

Ready Lap. JMFNM. 2020 · Zalel. UOG. 2003 · Parulekar. JUM. 1991 v0.12 · 19–40 wk
Lap and Zalel measure subtly different things: Lap = largest loop in coronal plane, Zalel = descending colon maximum internal diameter in parasagittal plane. The calculator surfaces both side-by-side; an inter-reference spread > 1.5 SD signals notable disagreement and is worth reconciling at the bedside.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and the colon lumen diameter. The z-score, percentile, severity, and side-by-side comparison across all three references update as you type.

Large bowel diameter vs GA
Lap 2020 · 20–40 wk · mean and 95% prediction interval
Mean (primary reference)
95% reference range
This measurement
Tips & tricks for measurement

Plane. Two valid approaches — pick one and be consistent on serial scans:

  • Coronal plane (Lap, Parulekar): identify the largest colon loop, measure its short-axis lumen.
  • Parasagittal plane (Zalel): identify the descending colon along the left flank, measure its maximum internal diameter.

What to look for. Colon is peripheral in the abdomen and follows the C-frame (cecum → ascending → transverse → descending → sigmoid → rectum). Haustral folds become visible from ~25 wk onward and are the most useful sign distinguishing colon from small bowel.

Caliper placement. Inner-to-inner bowel wall, perpendicular to the long axis of the loop. Avoid measuring across haustral indentations.

Meconium signal. Normal meconium is hypoechoic relative to fetal liver. Hyperechoic colon content = echogenic bowel (workup: CF, CMV, swallowed blood, aneuploidy, IUGR).

Common pitfalls.

  • A rounded, fluid-filled segment of transverse colon adjacent to the stomach can mimic a duodenal "double bubble" — scan in multiple planes to confirm continuity with the rest of the colon.
  • Cystic appearance of dilated sigmoid in late gestation can mimic an ovarian cyst or other pelvic mass.
  • Lap and Zalel can disagree by 1–2 SD because they measure different segments — the calculator surfaces this explicitly. Re-measure and pick the technique most consistent with prior scans.

When to escalate. Colon > 18 mm at term (Zalel/Nyberg max) or > 23 mm (Parulekar absolute upper bound) → suspect pathology. Workup: anorectal atresia (V/U-shape segment), Hirschsprung's, meconium plug, megacystis-microcolon-intestinal-hypoperistalsis. Detailed sonographic survey for VACTERL anomalies (92% concomitant in Harris series).

Equations & severity bands

Lap 2020 (primary). Cubic model in GA. Mean and 95% prediction interval at 2-week increments interpolated linearly. SD = (P97.5 − P2.5) / 3.92.

Zalel 2003 (descending colon). Per-week mean and 95% CI from cubic regression on 379 fetuses. Maximum at term: 18 mm. Equation in paper: Descending colon (mm) = 4.66 − 0.01·GA² + 0.0004·GA³ (calculator uses table values for accuracy).

Parulekar 1991 (categorical). 5-week GA buckets, average diameter + largest observed. Classical threshold: colon should not exceed 23 mm.

Z-score severity bands (Lap-derived):

  • z ≤ 2 — within reference
  • 2 < z ≤ 4 — mildly enlarged
  • 4 < z ≤ 8 — moderately enlarged
  • z > 8 — pathological range
References
  1. Lap CCMM et al. J Matern Fetal Neonatal Med 2020;33(4):633–638.
  2. Zalel Y, Perlitz Y, Gamzu R, Peleg D, Ben-Ami M. In-utero development of the fetal colon and rectum: sonographic evaluation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2003;21(2):161–164.
  3. Parulekar SG. Sonography of normal fetal bowel. J Ultrasound Med 1991;10(4):211–220.
Calculators › Fetal systems › Abdomen › Stomach

Fetal stomach diameter

Stomach visualisation, position, and diameter assessment. A formal published nomogram is not yet loaded into the project — the calculator surfaces classical clinical thresholds and qualitative findings, and will compute z-scores automatically once a reference table is added.

Reference paper pending Awaiting Goldstein 1987, Nagata 1990, or contemporary nomogram v0.12 · qualitative
The fetal stomach should be reliably visualised by 14 wk. Non-visualisation, the "double bubble" sign, marked distension, and right-sided position are each their own clinical entity — see "Tips & tricks" below for the full differential and management.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA. Diameter is optional. The assessment shows GA-appropriate visualisation expectations and clinical thresholds; once a reference paper is loaded into the project, z-scores will populate automatically.

Tips & tricks for measurement

Plane. Standard transverse plane of the upper abdomen — the same plane used for abdominal circumference. The stomach is a hypoechoic, fluid-filled sac in the left upper quadrant.

Visibility timeline. Stomach should be reliably visible by 14 wk. Re-scan in 30–45 min if not initially seen — physiological emptying is normal and the stomach refills within ~45 min.

Caliper placement (when measured). Maximum AP and longitudinal diameters in the standard transverse plane. Document size at maximum filling.

Position. Always confirm left-sided. Right-sided stomach = situs inversus or heterotaxy → dedicated cardiac and visceral situs survey.

Red flags & differential.

  • Persistent non-visualisation after 14 wk: esophageal atresia (often + polyhydramnios), microgastria, severe oligohydramnios, neuromuscular swallowing disorder, CDH with intrathoracic stomach.
  • "Double bubble" sign (fluid-filled stomach + adjacent fluid-filled duodenum): duodenal atresia / stenosis / annular pancreas. ~30% have trisomy 21.
  • Markedly distended (> 30 mm transverse, or visibly disproportionate to GA): consider gastric outlet or distal small-bowel obstruction.
  • Right-sided: situs inversus, heterotaxy syndromes — full cardiac survey.
  • Intrathoracic: left CDH (look for displaced cardiac axis, mediastinal shift).

Documentation. Note presence/absence, side, size category (small / appropriate / distended), and any double-bubble appearance. If non-visualised, repeat in 30–45 min before reporting.

References & status

Quantitative stomach reference paper not yet loaded in the project. To enable z-scoring, add one of:

  • Goldstein I, Reece EA, Yarkoni S, et al. Growth of the fetal stomach in normal pregnancies. Obstet Gynecol 1987;70(4):641–4.
  • Nagata S, Koyanagi T, Horimoto N, et al. Chronological development of the fetal stomach assessed using real-time ultrasound. Early Hum Dev 1990;22(1):15–22.
  • A contemporary multi-centre nomogram if you prefer.

Upload to the project and the data will be wired in to the same multi-reference engine that powers Small bowel, Large bowel, and Rectum.

Clinical thresholds shown above are drawn from standard ISUOG and SOGC guidance documents; they are qualitative pending the addition of a quantitative reference table.

Calculators › Fetal systems › Abdomen › Rectum

Fetal rectum diameter

Z-score and percentile of the fetal rectum anteroposterior diameter at the level of the bladder, against the Zalel 2003 reference (379 singleton pregnancies, cubic regression, 19–40 wk).

Ready Zalel. UOG. 2003 v0.12 · 19–40 wk
Maximum normal rectum AP diameter at term is 13 mm (Zalel 2003). A V- or U-shaped dilated segment in the lower fetal pelvis is highly suggestive of anorectal atresia; up to 92% of cases have additional anomalies (Harris 1987) — consider VACTERL workup.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and the rectum AP diameter measured at the level of the bladder (transverse plane). The z-score, percentile, and severity update as you type.

Rectum diameter vs GA
Zalel 2003 · 19–40 wk · mean and 95% CI
Mean (Zalel 2003)
95% confidence interval
This measurement
Tips & tricks for measurement

Plane. Transverse plane of the fetal pelvis at the level of the urinary bladder — same plane used to identify the bladder and umbilical arteries.

What to look for. The rectum is a tubular hypoechoic structure posterior to the bladder, midline. Identify the bladder first, then sweep posteriorly.

Caliper placement. Measure the anteroposterior (AP) diameter, inner-to-inner wall, at the widest part (Zalel's protocol). Average of three measurements is recommended for reproducibility.

Visibility timeline. Zalel achieved 100% visualisation from 19 wk onward — earlier and more reliably than the older Parulekar series, attributed to modern transducer resolution.

Common pitfalls.

  • Don't confuse a fluid-filled distended rectum with an ovarian cyst (in females) or other midline pelvic mass.
  • The dilated sigmoid colon can wrap into the lower pelvis and mimic rectal dilatation — confirm anatomic location relative to the bladder.
  • Hyperechoic content within the rectum or distal colon raises suspicion for anorectal atresia or meconium pathology.

Red flags.

  • V- or U-shaped dilated segment in the lower pelvis (Harris 1987) → anorectal atresia.
  • AP diameter > 13 mm at term (Zalel max): pathology likely — Hirschsprung's, anorectal malformation, megacystis-microcolon.
  • Concomitant anomalies in 92% of anorectal atresia cases (Harris) — survey for VACTERL: Vertebral, Anal, Cardiac, Tracheo-Esophageal, Renal, Limb. Karyotype/array offer.
  • Polyhydramnios increases pre-test probability of bowel obstruction.
Equations & severity bands

Zalel 2003 (primary). Per-week mean and 95% CI from cubic regression on 379 fetuses, 19–40 wk. SD derived as (P97.5 − P2.5) / 3.92. Equation in paper: Rectum AP (mm) = 2.88 − 0.014·GA² + 0.0002·GA³; the calculator uses table values directly for accuracy (the printed equation has a transcription artefact at low GA).

Z-score severity bands (consistent with Lap-derived bowel framework):

  • z ≤ 2 — within reference
  • 2 < z ≤ 4 — mildly enlarged · repeat in 2–4 wk
  • 4 < z ≤ 8 — moderately enlarged · serial monitoring; consider pediatric surgery
  • z > 8 — pathological range · urgent workup

Term threshold. Maximum normal rectum AP diameter at term: 13 mm.

References
  1. Zalel Y, Perlitz Y, Gamzu R, Peleg D, Ben-Ami M. In-utero development of the fetal colon and rectum: sonographic evaluation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2003;21(2):161–164.
  2. Harris RD, Nyberg DA, Mack LA, Weinberger E. Anorectal atresia: prenatal sonographic diagnosis. AJR Am J Roentgenol 1987;149(2):395–400.
Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Corpus callosum

Corpus callosum biometry

Multi-nomogram corpus callosum assessment: choose a measurement type (length variants, segment heights, or area). Results compare the selected value against published reference ranges with a Z-score and percentile.

Ready Cignini 2014 · Araujo Junior 2012 · Pashaj 2013 v0.6 · 18–41 wk (varies by reference)
Mid-sagittal plane is required. Beware the comma-shaped CC + cavum septi pellucidi complex on axial reconstructions — measure on a true sagittal view (transvaginal via anterior fontanelle, or transfrontal/transabdominal sagittal). Hypogenesis, dysgenesis, and partial agenesis can co-exist with normal length but abnormal segment thickness; review all four height segments before concluding.
Inputs
wk
d
mm
Assessment

Enter GA and a measurement value. Results update as you type.

Measurement vs gestational age
P5/P50/P95 reference
P50 (median)
P5 / P95
This measurement
How to measure

Plane. Strict mid-sagittal view of the fetal brain showing the entire corpus callosum (rostrum, genu, body, splenium) as a hypoechoic structure between the cingulate sulcus above and the cavum septi pellucidi below.

Approaches. Transvaginal via the anterior fontanelle (cephalic presentation) gives the highest resolution; transfrontal through the metopic suture (Visentin / Viñals) or direct transabdominal sagittal are alternatives. Avoid 3D reconstructions of the sagittal plane from an axial sweep — they can produce a comma-shaped CC + CSP artifact (Malinger 2006).

Length variants. CCL-C traces the convex curve and is always the longest. CCL-OO is a straight line from the most anterior genu to the most posterior splenium. CCL-II is a straight line between the innermost rostrum and innermost splenium (excludes the outer borders).

Segment heights. Measure each of the four segments at its thickest point, perpendicular to the long axis. All four heights together help characterise dysgenesis: e.g. preserved rostrum/genu with thinned body and splenium points to posterior partial agenesis.

Pitfalls. An anteroposterior cephalic position obscures the profile. Hyperechoic sulci above the CC and cavum septi pellucidi below are excluded from the measurement. Caliper resolution should be 0.1 mm.

References used for this measurement
    1. Pashaj S, Merz E, Wellek S. Biometry of the fetal corpus callosum by three-dimensional ultrasound. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;42:691–698. [Half-week P5/P50/P95 tables for 7 measurements; 466 fetuses; 18–41 wk]
    2. Araujo Junior E, Visentainer M, Simioni C, Ruano R, Nardozza LM, Moron AF. Reference values for the length and area of the fetal corpus callosum on 3-dimensional sonography using the transfrontal view. J Ultrasound Med 2012;31:205–212. [CC length & area tables; 293 fetuses; 20–33 wk]
    3. Cignini P, Padula F, Giorlandino M, et al. Reference charts for fetal corpus callosum length: a prospective cross-sectional study of 2950 fetuses. J Ultrasound Med 2014;33:1065–1078. [Largest CC length cohort; 19–37 wk]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Posterior fossa › BV / BT angles

    Posterior fossa angles — BV / BT

    Brainstem–vermis (BV) and brainstem–tentorium (BT) angles for differential diagnosis of upward rotation of the cerebellar vermis. Per Volpe 2012, a BV angle > 45° is strongly indicative of Dandy–Walker malformation; < 30° favours Blake’s pouch cyst; intermediate values are seen in vermian hypoplasia.

    Ready Volpe P et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2012;39:632–635 v1.2 · 19–28 wk
    Measurements must be obtained in a true mid-sagittal plane of the fetal brain. Categorical thresholds derive from a single retrospective series (31 abnormal + 80 control fetuses) and must be interpreted alongside the qualitative criteria for tentorium/torcular position and vermian morphology — particularly for vermian hypoplasia, where Volpe’s case numbers were small (n = 7).
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    °
    °
    Assessment

    Enter GA and BV angle. BT angle is optional and provides supporting context only.

    BV / BT angle distributions by diagnosis
    Volpe 2012 · controls (n = 80, 20–24 wk) and 31 pathological fetuses (19–28 wk)
    Mean ± 2 SD (each group)
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. A true mid-sagittal view of the fetal brain, obtained either by transvaginal sonography in vertex presentation or by 3D multiplanar reconstruction. The fastigium of the fourth ventricle, the brainstem, and the entire vermis (culmen through uvula) must be in view.

    BV angle. A line is drawn tangentially to the dorsal aspect of the brainstem. A second line is drawn tangentially to the ventral contour of the cerebellar vermis. The angle between them is the BV angle (Volpe Figure 1).

    BT angle. Same first line (dorsal brainstem). A third line is drawn tangentially to the tentorium cerebelli. The angle between brainstem and tentorium lines is the BT angle.

    Window. Volpe’s study was conducted at 19–28 weeks; outside this window the thresholds have not been formally validated, though earlier work in adjacent gestational ages suggests broadly similar patterns.

    Diagnostic thresholds & differential

    BV angle (primary discriminator):

    • < 18° — within normal range. Controls were always < 18°.
    • 18–30° — favours Blake’s pouch cyst. BPC fetuses were always < 30° (range 19–26°).
    • 30–45° — suggestive of vermian hypoplasia. VH fetuses showed intermediate values (24–40°). Caution: small case numbers (n = 7); diagnosis remains difficult.
    • > 45° — strongly suggestive of Dandy–Walker malformation. DWM fetuses were always > 45° (range 45–112°).

    BT angle (supportive only): Increased in pathology vs controls (controls always < 45°) but with substantial overlap between BPC, VH, and DWM. Useful as a secondary confirmation rather than an independent discriminator.

    Qualitative criteria remain essential. The tentorium and torcular are in normal position in Blake’s pouch cyst and vermian hypoplasia, but displaced upward in Dandy–Walker malformation. The vermis is intact in BPC, hypoplastic in VH, and normal-to-hypoplastic in DWM.

    About the reference
    1. Volpe P, Contro E, De Musso F, et al. Brainstem–vermis and brainstem–tentorium angles allow accurate categorization of fetal upward rotation of cerebellar vermis. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2012;39:632–635. [Primary · 31 anomalous (12 BPC, 12 DWM, 7 VH) + 80 controls · 19–28 wk]
    2. Ghi T, Contro E, De Musso F, et al. Normal morphometry of fetal posterior fossa at midtrimester: brainstem–tentorium angle and brainstem–vermis angle. Prenat Diagn 2012;32:1131–1135. [Companion paper — technique & reproducibility]
    3. Gandolfi Colleoni G, Contro E, Carletti A, et al. Prenatal diagnosis and outcome of fetal posterior fossa fluid collections. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2012;39:625–631. [Outcome series from same group]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Posterior fossa › Cerebellar vermis

    Cerebellar vermis biometry

    Mid-sagittal vermis measurements with side-by-side comparison of Malinger 2001 (transvaginal sonography, 21–40 wk) and Leibovitz 2014 (3D multiplanar reconstruction, 15–35 wk). Useful for diagnosis of vermian hypoplasia in syndromic and isolated settings.

    Ready Malinger G et al. Prenat Diagn 2001 · Leibovitz Z et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014 v1.2 · 15–40 wk
    True mid-sagittal plane is required — a parasagittal section inadvertently includes the cerebellar hemisphere and yields falsely-large values. AP definitions differ slightly between sources (Malinger: anterior central lobule to posterior tuber; Leibovitz APVD: fastigium to posterior tuber).
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA, pick a measurement type, and enter the value. Results show all applicable nomograms side-by-side.

    Vermis dimension vs gestational age
    P50 (median)
    P2.5 / P97.5 (± 2 SD)
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. A true mid-sagittal section of the fetal brain. The plane should pass through rostral and caudal vermian convexities and demonstrate the fastigium (Courchesne criterion). Trans-vaginal acquisition (in vertex presentation) or 3D multiplanar reconstruction give the most reliable mid-sagittal views.

    AP diameter (Malinger). Maximum distance between the most anterior portion of the central lobule and the most posterior portion of the tuber.

    Superoinferior diameter (CC / SIVD). Maximum distance between the most cranial portion of the culmen and the most caudal portion of the uvula.

    APVD (Leibovitz). Distance from the fastigial peak of the fourth ventricle to the posterior vermian edge (tuber). Note this is a different anchor anteriorly than Malinger’s AP, and is typically smaller.

    Circumference & area. Traced on the same mid-sagittal plane around the vermian silhouette (Malinger only).

    Choosing a reference

    Malinger 2001 — transvaginal sonography in 101 fetuses, 21–40 wk. Reports AP, CC, circumference, and surface area in mm/cm² by 2-week GA intervals. Use when applying the original anatomic definitions or when working from transvaginal images.

    Leibovitz 2014 — 3D multiplanar reconstruction in 334 fetuses, 15–35 wk (best 19–29 wk). Reports SIVD and APVD with mean ± 2 SD by GA week intervals. Use when working from 3D volumes; SIVD values tend to run slightly lower than Malinger CC because the strictly mid-sagittal plane excludes adjacent cerebellar hemisphere.

    When they disagree. If both refs are applicable and give discordant percentiles, prefer the one matching your acquisition method (2D TVS → Malinger; 3D-MPR → Leibovitz). Consistent < P2.5 results across both nomograms strengthens the case for true vermian hypoplasia.

    About the references
    1. Malinger G, Ginath S, Lerman-Sagie T, et al. The fetal cerebellar vermis: normal development as shown by transvaginal ultrasound. Prenat Diagn 2001;21:687–692. [Primary for AP, CC, circumference, area · 101 fetuses · 21–40 wk · TVS]
    2. Leibovitz Z, Shkolnik C, Krajden Haratz K, et al. Assessment of fetal midbrain and hindbrain in mid-sagittal cranial plane by three-dimensional multiplanar sonography. Part 1: comparison of new and established nomograms. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014;44:575–580. [Primary for SIVD/APVD · 334 fetuses · 15–35 wk · 3D-MPR]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Posterior fossa › Brainstem & midbrain

    Brainstem & midbrain biometry

    Mid-sagittal tectal length, AP midbrain diameter, AP pons diameter and AP fourth-ventricle diameter against Leibovitz 2014 (3D-MPR, 334 fetuses, 15–35 wk).

    Ready Leibovitz Z et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014;44:575–580 (Tables S1–S6) v1.2 · 15–35 wk
    Best feasibility window is 19–29 wk. Measurements rely on 3D multiplanar reconstruction in a true mid-sagittal plane; 2D parasagittal sections give inaccurate values. APDFV is the least reproducible measurement (intra-observer variation ~19%) — interpret extreme values cautiously.
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA, pick a measurement, and enter the value (in mm). Percentile + z-score returned against Leibovitz 2014 mean ± 2 SD.

    Brainstem dimension vs gestational age
    Mean (Leibovitz 2014)
    Mean ± 2 SD (~P2.5 / P97.5)
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Tectal length (TL). Distance between the superior and inferior edges of the tectal plate (distinct indentations above and below the superior and inferior colliculi).

    AP midbrain diameter (APMD). Longest distance between the anterior border of the midbrain and the posterior tectal edge, measured perpendicular to the TL.

    AP pons diameter (APPD). Maximum distance between the anterior pontine eminence and the anterior wall of the fourth ventricle, measured perpendicular to the longitudinal brainstem axis. The caliper is placed on the outer echogenic border of the pons (includes the leptomeninges).

    AP fourth-ventricle diameter (APDFV). Shortest distance from the fastigial peak perpendicular to the anterior wall of the fourth ventricle. Highest measurement variability among the four parameters — use cautiously.

    About the reference

    All four nomograms derive from a single prospective cross-sectional study of 334 low-risk singleton pregnancies (16–35 wk) using 3D multiplanar reconstruction. Adequate visualisation was achieved in 65–91% of cases depending on parameter; the feasibility-best window was 19–29 wk. Pearson’s correlations with GA: TL 0.79, APMD 0.88, APPD 0.91, SIVD 0.95, APVD 0.88, APDFV 0.88.

    1. Leibovitz Z, Shkolnik C, Krajden Haratz K, et al. Assessment of fetal midbrain and hindbrain in mid-sagittal cranial plane by three-dimensional multiplanar sonography. Part 1: comparison of new and established nomograms. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014;44:575–580. [Primary · Tables S1–S6 tabulated centiles by GA week interval]
    2. Leibovitz Z, et al. Part 2: application of nomograms to fetuses with posterior fossa malformations. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2014;44:581–587. [Companion paper — pathology application]
    3. Barkovich AJ, Millen KJ, Dobyns WB. A developmental and genetic classification for midbrain-hindbrain malformations. Brain 2009;132:3199–3230. [Classification of MB-HB anomalies]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › CNS › Posterior fossa › PF morphometry (Paladini)

    Posterior fossa morphometry (Paladini)

    Four-parameter mid-sagittal characterisation per Paladini 2006 (51 normal + 20 abnormal fetuses; 3D-US static VCI). Quantifies tentorial upward displacement (tentoroclivus angle, TC), vermian upward rotation (tentorovermian and clivovermian angles, TV & CV), and vermian hypoplasia (VD/BPD ratio). The combined fingerprint distinguishes normal · megacisterna magna · Dandy–Walker variant · Dandy–Walker malformation. Complementary to the BV/BT angles calculator (which uses different reference lines).

    Ready Paladini D, Volpe P. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2006;27:482–489 v1.5 · 18–37 wk
    All four parameters are measured on the mid-sagittal plane of the fetal head, ideally from a 3D acquisition through the posterior fontanelle so the ultrasound beam is near-perpendicular to the tentorium. Static VCI rendering (2–3 mm slice thickness) sharpens the tentorial and vermian edges. The small n in the abnormal groups in Paladini’s series (DW n=5, DWv n=8, MCM n=7) means the reference SDs are wide; treat the fingerprint as supportive rather than diagnostic.
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    °
    °
    °
    mm
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and any subset of the 4 parameters (TC, TV, CV, VD/BPD). Returns per-parameter z-scores vs Paladini’s normal group + a categorical fingerprint match.

    Reference values per group (Paladini 2006 Table 1)
    GroupnTC (°)TV (°)CV (°)VD/BPD
    Normal514.0 ± 4.255.6 ± 9.047.8 ± 7.322.4 ± 2.9
    MCM711.4 ± 13.057.4 ± 11.854.1 ± 5.824.0 ± 2.1
    DWv88.3 ± 9.39.9 ± 21.3−0.6 ± 11.718.9 ± 2.1
    DWM545.8 ± 22.08.9 ± 72.3−13.2 ± 69.115.1 ± 3.0

    All values mean ± SD. MCM megacisterna magna · DWv Dandy–Walker variant · DWM Dandy–Walker malformation. When the vermis is rotated so its long axis crosses the tentorium or clivus line, TV/CV are recorded as negative.

    Anatomical landmarks

    Tentorium (line a-a′). Drawn from the posterior end of the corpus callosum to the tentorium’s attachment on the inner aspect of the occipital bone.

    Vermian long axis (line b-b′). Connects the uppermost point of the culmen to the caudal edge of the uvula.

    Clivus (line c-c′). The straight bony line of the basisphenoid/basiocciput.

    Tentoroclivus angle (TC). Between tentorium and clivus. Increased in DWM (gross tentorial upward displacement).

    Tentorovermian angle (TV). Between tentorium and vermian axis. Normal ≈ 55°, open posteriorly. Reduced (or negative) in DWv and DWM due to upward vermian rotation.

    Clivovermian angle (CV). Between clivus and vermian axis. Normal ≈ 48°, open anteriorly. Reduced (or negative) in DWv and DWM.

    VD/BPD ratio. Normalises vermian craniocaudal diameter for fetal size. The only Paladini measurement with a positive linear correlation with GA (r = 0.922). Reduced in vermian hypoplasia.

    About the reference

    Paladini 2006 acquired 3D volumes from the posterior fontanelle and used static VCI multiplanar imaging (2–3 mm slice thickness) to enhance edge contrast over conventional 2D. Inter- and intraobserver Spearman correlations for the three angles were 0.91–0.95; for VD/BPD ratio, 0.87 (inter) and 0.91 (intra). MRI–US correlation in the 7 cases with same-day scans was 0.94–0.99 across all four parameters.

    1. Paladini D, Volpe P. Posterior fossa and vermian morphometry in the characterization of fetal cerebellar abnormalities: a prospective three-dimensional ultrasound study. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2006;27:482–489. [Primary · 51 normal + 20 abnormal · 3D-US static VCI]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › Genitalia › Fetal penile length

    Fetal penile length

    Penile length percentile vs Zalel 2001 (419 normal male fetuses, 14–38 wk, transvaginal ≤17 wk and transabdominal ≥18 wk). Measurements below the reference range are suspicious for micropenis, hypospadias, or ambiguous genitalia — warranting detailed evaluation for chromosomal, syndromic, or endocrine causes.

    Ready Zalel Y et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2001;17:129–131 v1.3 · 14–38 wk (male)
    Apply only when the fetal phenotype is unambiguously male. Reduced penile length on a single measurement is not diagnostic — isolated < P5 mandates rescan, dedicated genitalia survey, and consideration of karyotype/microarray, 17-OHP, and detailed cardiac/CNS imaging given the broad differential (chromosomal, syndromic, hormonal).
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and penile length. Results show percentile, z-score, and clinical flag against Zalel 2001 reference.

    Penile length vs gestational age
    Zalel 2001 · 14–38 wk · mean & 90% prediction interval (P5–P95)
    Mean
    P5 / P95 (90% prediction interval)
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. Transverse view of the fetal perineum/genitalia. Up to 17 weeks, transvaginal sonography substantially improves visualisation; from 18 weeks transabdominal is usually adequate. The phallus must be imaged in true profile with a clearly defined glans.

    Caliper placement. Measure from the edge of the scrotum (junction with the penile shaft) to the tip of the glans. Do not include the scrotum in the measurement — this is a common cause of over-estimation.

    Reproducibility. Zalel reports intra- and inter-observer coefficients of variation of 5% and 6.8% respectively, with three replicate measurements averaged. Repeat measurements and avoid measuring during a fetal voiding episode (urethral distension can confound the caliper landmarks).

    Pitfalls. Late in pregnancy the legs may obscure the perineum — wait for a fetal repositioning. Hypospadias (Figure 1b in Zalel) typically shows a short, wide, ventrally curved (“tulip”) phallus and is often associated with scrotal asymmetry.

    Clinical interpretation & differential

    Thresholds:

    • z < −2.5 — micropenis range (clinical threshold per Feldman 1975, adopted by Zalel 2001). Requires investigation regardless of associated findings.
    • −2.5 ≤ z < −1.645 (below P5) — below the 90% prediction interval. Suspicious for micropenis or ambiguous genitalia; rescan and proceed with detailed work-up.
    • −1.645 ≤ z ≤ 1.645 — within the reference range (P5–P95).
    • z > 1.645 (above P95) — atypically large; clinical significance limited but consider macrosomia and rare androgen-excess states.

    Differential for micropenis (z < −2.5): chromosomal (Zalel reported a trisomy 13 case identified through micropenis), syndromic (> 70 syndromes feature ambiguous genitalia / micropenis per Smith’s; consider Prader-Willi, Smith-Lemli-Opitz, Kallmann), hormonal (hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism, partial androgen insensitivity), or structural (cloacal anomalies). Recommend detailed neurosonography, cardiac scan, karyotype/microarray, and referral to medical genetics and paediatric endocrinology.

    Hypospadias: may show normal length on this nomogram — the diagnosis depends on the shape of the phallus and the position of the urethral meatus rather than length alone. Look for the “tulip sign” (curved phallus with bifid glans).

    About the reference

    Zalel 2001 used a square-root transform on penile length to achieve constant variance across gestation: √(PL mm) = 0.277 + 0.121 × GA (wk), R = 0.967. Z-scores in this calculator are computed on the √-scale, then back-transformed to percentile. The displayed reference band uses the paper’s published 90% prediction intervals (Table 1) rather than ± 2 SD, so the band edges represent P5 and P95.

    1. Zalel Y, Pinhas-Hamiel O, Lipitz S, Mashiach S, Achiron R. The development of the fetal penis — an in utero sonographic evaluation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2001;17:129–131. [Primary · 419 male fetuses · 14–38 wk · TVS ≤17 wk + TA ≥18 wk]
    2. Feldman KW, Smith DW. Penile length. J Pediatr 1975;86:395–398. [Neonatal reference — established the 2.5 SD micropenis threshold]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › Genitalia › Scrotal circumference

    Fetal scrotal circumference

    Scrotal circumference vs Achiron 1998 (210 normal male fetuses, 14–37 wk, transvaginal ≤17 wk + transabdominal ≥18 wk). Useful for confirming normal scrotal development, flagging gross hypoplasia (anorchia, severe cryptorchidism, ambiguous genitalia), and identifying scrotal enlargement (hydrocele, inguinoscrotal hernia, scrotal pathology).

    Ready Achiron R et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;11:242–245 v1.5 · 14–37 wk
    This calculator uses a percent-of-expected-mean flagging system rather than precise percentiles — the original Achiron 1998 paper publishes a CI of the mean (not a prediction interval for individual measurements) and the residual SD is not reported, so a true percentile cannot be computed. Clinically appropriate for the gross-abnormality use case the measurement actually serves.
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and scrotal circumference. Returns expected mean and percent-of-expected categorical flag.

    How to measure

    Plane. Axial view of the fetal perineum/genitalia, the scrotum fully in plane. Transvaginal sonography from 14 to 17 wk; transabdominal from 18 wk onward.

    Caliper placement. Electronic trace around the outer edge of the scrotum. Three replicate measurements averaged.

    Pitfalls. Compression by fetal thighs or the cord can flatten the scrotal contour. Re-attempt after fetal repositioning.

    Clinical interpretation

    Markedly small (< 50% of expected). Consider anorchia, severe cryptorchidism with scrotal hypoplasia, ambiguous genitalia, or genetic syndromes (Smith\u2019s catalogues > 70 syndromes with scrotal/genital anomalies). Correlate with penile length, testicular descent timing, kidneys, and karyotype/microarray.

    Small (50–75% of expected). Borderline. Repeat measurement; correlate with other genital measurements.

    Within expected range (75–125% of expected). Reassuring.

    Enlarged (125–150% of expected). Consider hydrocele (common, often resolves spontaneously) or inguinoscrotal hernia (look for peristalsing bowel within the scrotum).

    Markedly enlarged (> 150% of expected). More likely hernia, hydrocele with significant fluid, or rarely scrotal mass/tumour. Detailed scan recommended.

    About the reference

    Achiron 1998 fitted an exponential growth model: SC(mm) = exp(1.55 + 0.09 × GA(wk)), R = 0.96. Note: the paper’s printed equation has a typo (intercept 1.05 instead of 1.55) — the corrected intercept reproduces Table 1 values within rounding. The residual SD of individual measurements was not published, so this calculator uses a percent-of-expected-mean classification rather than a true percentile.

    1. Achiron R, Pinhas-Hamiel O, Zalel Y, Rotstein Z, Lipitz S. Development of fetal male gender: prenatal sonographic measurement of the scrotum and evaluation of testicular descent. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;11:242–245. [Primary · 210 fetuses · 14–37 wk]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › Genitalia › Testicular descent

    Testicular descent

    Categorical timing assessment of intrascrotal testicular descent per Achiron 1998 (210 male fetuses). Descent does not occur before 25 weeks; the second ‘transinguinal’ phase is androgen-dependent and occurs 26–35 wk. By 32–33 wk, 97% of fetuses have bilateral testes within the scrotum. Cryptorchidism is the most frequent endocrine-gland anomaly (3.4% at term) and prenatal recognition supports early postnatal follow-up.

    Ready Achiron R et al. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;11:242–245 v1.5 · 14–35 wk
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    Assessment

    Enter GA and observed descent status. Returns expected nomogram and flags delayed descent.

    Descent timing reference (Achiron 1998 Table 2)
    GA (wk)nNoneUnilateralBilateral
    14–24114100%0%0%
    251070%20%10%
    261010%70%20%
    2780%12%88%
    28–29200%20%80%
    30–31190%11%89%
    32+310%3%97%
    Clinical interpretation

    Two-phase descent model. The first phase (transabdominal migration, 10–15 wk) is mediated by Müllerian inhibiting substance and occurs invisibly. The second phase (transinguinal migration, 26–35 wk) is androgen-dependent (calcitonin gene-related peptide from the genitofemoral nerve) and is the phase visible on ultrasound.

    Cryptorchidism. 3.4% at term, falling to 0.8–1% at 1 year (spontaneous late descent). Risk of malignancy is 10–40-fold higher than in normal men; histologic damage to germ cells is largely irreversible by age 1–2. Prenatal flagging supports paediatric urology follow-up and early treatment.

    Failure of descent by 32+ wk — expected unilateral or bilateral. Refer for postnatal paediatric examination; re-check at first newborn visit.

    Bilateral non-descent in a karyotypically male fetus, particularly with micropenis or scrotal hypoplasia. Consider hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism, partial androgen insensitivity, Prader-Willi spectrum, 5α-reductase deficiency. Endocrine and genetic referral postnatally.

    About the reference
    1. Achiron R, Pinhas-Hamiel O, Zalel Y, Rotstein Z, Lipitz S. Development of fetal male gender: prenatal sonographic measurement of the scrotum and evaluation of testicular descent. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;11:242–245. [Primary · 210 fetuses · 14–35 wk]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › Abdomen › Fetal liver length

    Fetal liver length

    Right-lobe liver length (right hemidiaphragm to tip of the right lobe) against the Vintzileos 1985 nomogram (221 measurements in 100 women, 20–39 wk; spline regression, r² = 0.86). In Vintzileos’ series, the fetal liver was the earliest measurement to become abnormally small in asymmetric growth restriction and abnormally large in macrosomia — reduction often preceded abdominal-circumference change.

    Ready Vintzileos AM et al. Obstet Gynecol 1985;66:477–480 v1.4 · 20–39 wk
    Measure on a longitudinal scan with the transducer parallel to the long axis of the aorta. Three replicate measurements (within a 2 mm spread) are averaged per Vintzileos. Do not measure when the fetal back is anterior or the right side is dependent — rib shadowing falsifies the caliper landmarks. Original data is sparse beyond 38 wk; use cautiously near term.
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and right-lobe liver length. Returns percentile + z-score against Vintzileos 1985.

    Liver length vs gestational age
    Vintzileos 1985 · 20–39 wk · mean ± 2 SD (~P2.5/P97.5)
    Mean
    Mean ± 2 SD
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. Longitudinal section through the fetal trunk. First identify the descending aorta in a longitudinal plane, then slide the transducer parallel until both the right hemidiaphragm and the tip of the right hepatic lobe are visible in a single plane.

    Caliper placement. First caliper on the right hemidiaphragm; second caliper on the most caudal point of the right lobe of the liver. Measurement is along the cranio-caudal axis.

    Replicates. Vintzileos repeated measurements until three were reproducible within 2 mm and averaged the three.

    Pitfalls. Fetal back-anterior or right-side-dependent position falsifies the caliper landmarks because of rib shadowing — skip the measurement and re-attempt after fetal movement.

    Growth rate. Per Vintzileos: 1.2 mm/wk between 20 and 30 wk, then accelerates to 1.7 mm/wk between 31 and 41 wk. The third-trimester acceleration makes liver length particularly useful for detecting late-onset growth abnormalities, where BPD and FL growth rates plateau.

    Clinical interpretation

    Below mean − 2 SD (P< 2.5). Vintzileos reported that all 10 growth-retarded fetuses in their series (birth weight < 10th centile) had liver lengths > 2 SD below the mean — sometimes when the AC was still within normal range. Reduced liver length is consistent with asymmetric IUGR, with early compromise of liver mass from glycogen depletion.

    Above mean + 2 SD (P> 97.5). All 6 macrosomic fetuses (birth weight > 90th centile) in Vintzileos’ series had liver lengths > 2 SD above the mean. Reasonable trigger to assess maternal glycaemic status.

    Hepatomegaly without macrosomia. Important sign in Rh sensitisation / erythroblastosis fetalis — in three of Vintzileos’ severe-Rh cases, liver enlargement preceded ascites or oedema while AC remained normal. Also seen in TORCH infections, congenital metabolic disease, and congestive heart failure.

    Alternative axis. Vintzileos also reported a linear relationship with AC: Liver length (mm) = 6.32 + 1.34 × AC (cm) (r = 0.91). Useful when AC and liver length disagree — a normal liver length for the actual AC argues for symmetric IUGR rather than asymmetric.

    About the reference
    1. Vintzileos AM, Neckles S, Campbell WA, Andreoli JW Jr, Kaplan BM, Nochimson DJ. Fetal liver ultrasound measurements during normal pregnancy. Obstet Gynecol 1985;66:477–480. [Primary · 221 measurements, 100 women · 20–41 wk · spline regression at 30 wk, r² = 0.86]

    Data at GA 40 (n = 1) and GA 41 (n = 2 with an implausible mean drop) have been excluded from this implementation; reference window truncated to 20–39 wk for reliability.

    Calculators › Fetal systems › Chest › Fetal thymus

    Fetal thymus

    Two measurement options: 3D-US volume against Li/Bahtiyar 2010 (238 singletons, r = 0.989) for centres with VOCAL capability, or 2D transverse diameter against Cho 2007 (352/376 fetuses, r² = 0.86) for routine 2D-US in the three-vessel view. Reduced thymus is a documented marker for conotruncal CHDs (Chaoui 2002: sensitivity 90%, specificity 98.5% for 22q11.2 microdeletion in fetal cardiac defects), with the deletion concentrated in IAA (67%), absent pulmonary valve syndrome (50%), truncus arteriosus (33%), and tetralogy of Fallot (9%).

    Ready Li/Bahtiyar 2010 (3D volume) · Cho 2007 (2D diameter) · Chaoui 2002 (22q11.2 marker) v1.5 · 17–38 wk
    Both measurements taken at the three-vessel view (or 3VT view) with fetal back posterior, sternum closest to the transducer. 2D transverse diameter: line cursor perpendicular to the sternum–spine line, between the two lung interfaces (Cho 2007). 3D volume: VOCAL with 45° sweep angle, manually trace the thymus on 10 sagittal sub-slices (Li 2010). Twins use the singleton nomogram for both (Li P = 0.85).
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and 3D thymus volume (mL). Returns percentile, z-score, and CHD/22q11.2 advisory if below reference.

    Thymus vs gestational age
    17–38 wk · mean ± 2 SD
    Mean (linear regression)
    Mean ± 2 SD
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. Begin with the 3-vessel or 3-vessel-and-trachea view (pulmonary artery, aorta, SVC; trachea to the right). The thymus appears as a homogeneous quadrangular structure in the anterior superior mediastinum, ventral to the great vessels, between the two lungs. Aim for the fetal back posterior to the transducer so the sternum is closest.

    Echogenicity. Similar to or slightly more echogenic than lung at 19–22 wk, becoming less echogenic than lung after 22 wk (Li 2010).

    3D acquisition. Sweep angle 45°. Truncated sector centred on the thymus to maximise resolution. Store volume.

    VOCAL contouring. Open the volume in VOCAL (or equivalent organ-volume tool), then manually trace the thymus border on each of 10 sagittal sub-slices. Software computes volume automatically. Repeat 3 times and average.

    Failure points. Visualisation fails (~5% in Li 2010) when ribs, extremities, or sternum shadow the gland; unfavourable fetal position; or maternal habitus. Re-attempt after fetal movement.

    Clinical interpretation & 22q11.2

    Reduced TV (< P5). In Li 2010, fetuses with congenital heart defects had significantly lower TV than controls (P < 0.05) after adjusting for EFW — the link is strongest for conotruncal lesions (truncus arteriosus, TGA, double-outlet right ventricle, interrupted aortic arch type B, pulmonary atresia with VSD, tetralogy of Fallot).
