The mechanism is shared embryologic origin: pharyngeal-arch neural crest cells contribute to both thymic stroma and conotruncal septation.

    22q11.2 microdeletion. Absent or hypoplastic thymus is one of the most common findings in confirmed 22q11.2 cases (Chaoui 2002). In the setting of a conotruncal CHD, a small TV strengthens the case for microdeletion testing (FISH or microarray) — particularly when a fetal echo finds an at-risk lesion.

    Other causes of thymic hypoplasia/aplasia. DiGeorge syndrome, chondrodysplasia, severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID), intrauterine growth restriction, chorioamnionitis.

    Twins. Per Li 2010, twin and singleton TVs are statistically similar when adjusted for EFW (P = 0.85) — the singleton nomogram applies directly.

    About the reference

    Li 2010 fitted a linear regression: TV (mL) = 0.4639 × GA(wk) − 7.8504 (r = 0.989). The SD was modelled per the Royston & Wright method as SD = 0.3754 + 0.0004 × GA. The chart shows mean ± 2 SD (~P2.5/P97.5); the paper’s Table 2 reports P5 and P95 directly.

    1. Li L, Bahtiyar MO, Buhimschi CS, Zou L, Zhou Q-C, Copel JA. Assessment of the fetal thymus by two- and three-dimensional ultrasound during normal human gestation and in fetuses with congenital heart defects. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2011;37:404–409. [Primary · 3D volume · 238 normal singletons + 64 normal twins + 19 CHD · 17–39 wk · 3D VOCAL]
    2. Cho JY, Min JY, Lee Y-H, McCrindle B, Hornberger LK, Yoo S-J. Diameter of the normal fetal thymus on ultrasound. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;29:634–638. [Primary · 2D transverse diameter · 352/376 fetuses · 19–38 wk · regression diameter (cm) = 0.15 × GA − 1.59, SD 0.319, r² = 0.86]
    3. Chaoui R, Kalache K, Heling K, Tennstedt C, Bommer C, Körner H. Absent or hypoplastic thymus on ultrasound: a marker for deletion 22q11.2 in fetal cardiac defects. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2002;20:546–552. [Pioneering work on the CHD / 22q11.2 / thymus connection]
    4. Royston P, Wright E. How to construct ‘normal ranges’ for fetal variables. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;11:30–38. [Statistical methodology used by Li 2010]
    Calculators › Fetal systems › Face › Orbit & lens biometry

    Fetal orbit & lens biometry

    Multi-measurement assessment of orbital and lens size against the Goldstein 1998 tabulated centiles, with a Dilmen 2002 expected-mean cross-check. Used to support diagnosis of microphthalmia / anophthalmia, macrophthalmia, and disproportionate lens-to-orbit growth in the context of suspected craniofacial syndromes or intrauterine infections.

    Ready Goldstein 1998 (primary) · Dilmen 2002 (cross-check) v0.17 · 14–36 wk (Goldstein)
    Use a transverse axial plane orthogonal to the BPD plane, with both orbits symmetrical and the lens visible inside each orbit. Goldstein and Dilmen both place calipers mid-echogenicity to mid-echogenicity of the latero-orbital and middle-orbital margins (orbit) or the lens borders (lens). This is not interchangeable with outer-to-outer or inner-to-inner techniques. Microphthalmia is conventionally defined as ocular diameter ≥ 2 SD below the mean; the calculator flags < P5 in that direction.
    Inputs
    wk
    d
    mm
    Assessment

    Enter GA and a measurement value. Results update as you type.

    Measurement vs gestational age
    Goldstein 1998 (tabulated centiles)
    P50 (median)
    P10 / P90
    This measurement
    How to measure

    Plane. Coronal or transverse axial section of the fetal face oriented at 90° to the BPD plane. Both orbits should appear as paired echolucent circles, ideally symmetrical, with the lens visible as a hyperechoic ring with a hypoechoic centre in the anterior aspect of each orbit (ISUOG mid-trimester guideline 2022).

    Caliper convention (Goldstein / Dilmen).

    • Orbit: mid-echogenicity of the latero-orbital margin to mid-echogenicity of the middle-orbital margin (i.e., transverse diameter of the bony orbit).
    • Lens: mid-echogenicity to mid-echogenicity of the lens borders along its anteroposterior axis, in the same plane.
    • Circumference and area in this calculator are derived geometrically as 2π·r and π·r² respectively, matching the convention used by Goldstein and Dilmen.

    Quick screening rule (Ondeck 2018). The width of one orbit should approximately equal the inter-orbital distance — i.e., one orbit width should fit comfortably between the other two. Asymmetry, or marked deviation from this 1:1:1 rule, prompts formal biometry. Note that this calculator measures orbital size and does not compute interocular or binocular distance.

    Clinical thresholds. Microphthalmia is conventionally defined as ocular diameter ≥ 2 SD below the mean for GA (Burns 2013, Verma 2007). The calculator flags < P5 (≈ Z < −1.65) and > P95 in the result card. Anophthalmia is qualitative — total absence of the globe — and not detectable by biometry alone.

    Tips & tricks
    • Plane selection matters. A slightly oblique plane will under-measure both orbits symmetrically; an asymmetric plane can falsely suggest unilateral microphthalmia. Verify both orbits are sectioned at their largest diameter before measuring.
    • Bony shadowing. The frontal bone or maxilla can cast acoustic shadows that obscure the medial orbital margin. Angle the probe to bring both margins clear of shadow.
    • Lens visualisation. The lens is best seen at 14–17 wk transvaginally and from 18 wk transabdominally (Goldstein 1998). Increased lens echogenicity, loss of the central hypoechoic core, or thickened irregular margins suggests congenital cataract — this is a qualitative diagnosis, not a biometric one (Ondeck 2018).
    • Lens-to-orbit ratio. Goldstein reported a falling lens/orbit diameter ratio with GA: ~0.45 at 14 wk → ~0.35 at 36 wk. Marked deviation may signal disproportionate growth (e.g., microphthalmia with normal lens, or PHPV / PFV with lens enlargement).
    • Suspected microphthalmia. Consider associated anomalies: holoprosencephaly, agenesis of the corpus callosum, midline brain malformations, and chromosomal aneuploidies (trisomy 13, SOX2-related disorders, CHARGE, Walker-Warburg). Detailed neurosonography and genetic counselling are recommended.
    • Hypotelorism / hypertelorism. These are diagnoses of inter-orbital distance, not orbital size, and are not assessed here. Use IOD/BOD reference tables (Mayden 1982, Trout 1994, Robinson 2008) on the same axial plane.
    About the references
    1. Goldstein I, Tamir A, Zimmer EZ, Itskovitz-Eldor J. Growth of the fetal orbit and lens in normal pregnancies. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1998;12:175–179. [Primary · 349 healthy fetuses · transvaginal 14–17 wk + transabdominal 18–36 wk · tabulated P10/P25/P50/P75/P90 for orbital and lens diameter, circumference, and area · linear growth functions r ≥ 0.89]
    2. Dilmen G, Köktener A, Turhan NO, Tez S. Growth of the fetal lens and orbit. Int J Gynecol Obstet 2002;76:267–271. [Cross-check · 335 fetuses · 15–40 wk · linear regressions (R² 0.89–0.95) · same caliper technique as Goldstein · used here as an independent expected-mean comparator]
    3. Ondeck CL, Pretorius D, McCaulley J, et al. Ultrasonographic prenatal imaging of fetal ocular and orbital abnormalities. Surv Ophthalmol 2018;63:745–753. [Qualitative review of ocular anomalies including cataract, hypotelorism, hypertelorism, dacryocystocele, microphthalmia, anophthalmia, septo-optic dysplasia]
    4. Burns NS, Iyer RS, Robinson AJ, Chapman T. Diagnostic imaging of fetal and pediatric orbital abnormalities. AJR 2013;201:W797–W808. [Compiled fetal MRI biometry from Robinson 2008, Paquette 2009, Li 2010 for BOD/IOD/OD/LD; cited here for the 2-SD definition of microphthalmia and the cross-modality context]
    Calculators › Growth trajectory

    Growth trajectory

    Plot serial EFW measurements on a Hadlock 1991 percentile chart to visualise growth velocity and percentile tracking. Default EFW formula is Hadlock-3 (HC + AC + FL); switchable to Hadlock-1, -2, or -4 above.

    Ready Hadlock FP et al. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1985 (formula) · Hadlock FP et al. Radiology 1991 (percentiles) v0.5 · Singleton
    Trajectory interpretation: tracking parallel to a percentile is reassuring; significant percentile crossing (≥ 2 quartiles) or static interval growth raises concern for FGR or LGA depending on direction. Confirm dating before reading trajectories.
    Pregnancy anchor & formula

    If you don't know the EDD, leave it blank and enter GA manually for each exam below.

    Exams
    Trajectory
    EFW trajectory vs gestational age
    Hadlock 1991 in-utero growth standard · 5 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 95 percentiles
    50th centile
    10th / 90th
    5th / 95th
    Exam measurement
    Trajectory
    Calculators › Twin NT discordance

    Twin nuchal translucency discordance

    Inter-twin NT discordance in monochorionic and dichorionic twins. NT discordance ≥ 20% is an early predictor of twin–twin transfusion syndrome and of adverse outcome. Per-fetus NT is interpreted exactly as in singletons — what is twin-specific is the discordance and how risk is aggregated.

    Ready ISUOG 2016 · SOGC No. 440 2023 v0.1
    This tool reports inter-twin NT discordance and flags the ≥ 20% TTTS-predictor threshold. It does not compute aneuploidy risk — combined first-trimester screening risk should be calculated in an accredited FMF risk engine (per-pregnancy in monochorionic twins, per-fetus in dichorionic twins).
    Inputs
    mm
    mm
    Result

    Enter both NT measurements and tap Calculate to see inter-twin discordance and the TTTS-predictor flag.

    How to interpret this result

    NT discordance is calculated as (larger NT − smaller NT) ÷ larger NT × 100.

    • NT discordance ≥ 20% is found in around 25% of monochorionic twins; in that group the risk of early intrauterine death or development of severe TTTS exceeds 30%. ISUOG advises that twin pregnancies with NT discordance ≥ 20% be discussed with a fetal medicine expert.
    • NT discordance < 20% carries a risk of TTTS or early loss below ~10%. The marker's overall predictive accuracy for TTTS is modest (sensitivity ≈ 52–64%, specificity ≈ 78–80%) — a normal result does not exclude TTTS, and biweekly ultrasound surveillance of monochorionic twins from 16 weeks remains essential regardless.
    • An increased NT in either twin (≥ 95th centile for CRL, or commonly ≥ 3.5 mm) still warrants the usual singleton work-up: offer of diagnostic testing, detailed early anatomy, and serial fetal echocardiography.
    Aneuploidy risk in twins — per-pregnancy vs per-fetus
    • Monochorionic twins are (almost always) monozygotic and share a karyotype: the trisomy-21 risk from combined first-trimester screening is calculated per pregnancy, using the average of the two NT measurements together with maternal age and serum markers.
    • Dichorionic twins are mostly dizygotic with independent karyotypes: risk is calculated per fetus.
    • Vanished twin — if a measurable fetal pole remains, serum free β-hCG and PAPP-A are unreliable; risk should be estimated from NT and maternal age alone.
    • cfDNA screening is an option in twins; detection rates for trisomy 21 are slightly lower than in singletons and the published twin experience is smaller. Invasive testing carries a modestly higher loss rate than in singletons, and counselling is best done in a centre experienced with multiples.
    • Discordance for an anomaly or aneuploidy is rare but possible even in monochorionic pairs (heterokaryotypic monochorionic twins) — double amniocentesis is often preferred when invasive testing is indicated.
    Audit & reference
    Discordance formula
    (NTlarger − NTsmaller) ÷ NTlarger × 100
    TTTS-predictor threshold
    NT discordance ≥ 20% (ISUOG 2016)
    Sources
    Khalil A, Rodgers M, Baschat A, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: role of ultrasound in twin pregnancy. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2016;47:247–263.
    Lee H-S, Abbasi N, Van Mieghem T, et al. SOGC Guideline No. 440: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancies. J Obstet Gynaecol Can 2023;45:587–606.
    Kagan KO, Gazzoni A, Sepulveda-Gonzalez G, et al. Discordance in NT thickness in the prediction of severe TTTS. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2007;29:527–532.
    Scope
    Discordance + categorical flag only; does not compute screening risk
    Version
    v0.1 (pending institutional review)
    Calculators › Twin growth trajectory

    Twin growth trajectory

    Plot serial EFW for both twins on the chorionicity-specific Fetal Medicine Foundation 2024 twin charts, with inter-twin EFW discordance and a parallel singleton-percentile view. EFW is locked to Hadlock-3 (HC + AC + FL) — the formula the FMF twin reference was derived with.

    Ready Wright A et al. UOG 2024 (FMF twin charts) · Hadlock-3 1985 · FMF singleton charts 2018 v0.1 · Twin
    Twins grow more slowly than singletons from the third trimester onwards, more so in monochorionic pairs — assessed against singleton charts, 30–50% of normal twins are labelled SGA at term. EFW discordance ≥ 25% (≥ 20% as a pragmatic prompt) and an abnormal umbilical artery Doppler are independent risk factors for adverse perinatal outcome and warrant referral.
    Pregnancy anchor & chorionicity

    EFW is calculated with Hadlock-3 (HC + AC + FL) for every exam — the FMF twin charts were built on this formula. If you don't know the EDD, leave it blank and enter GA manually for each exam.

    Exams
    Trajectory & discordance
    EFW trajectory vs gestational age — twin percentile scale
    FMF 2024 chorionicity-specific twin standard · 3 / 10 / 50 / 90 / 97 percentiles
    50th centile (twin)
    10th / 90th
    3rd / 97th
    Twin A
    Twin B
    Same measurements on the singleton-percentile scale
    FMF 2018 singleton standard — shows how far twin growth diverges from singleton expectation
    50th centile (singleton)
    10th / 90th
    Twin A
    Twin B
    How to interpret this result

    Each twin's EFW is plotted against the chorionicity-specific FMF 2024 twin standard and, in parallel, against the FMF singleton standard.

    • Twin-scale percentile — the appropriate reference for deciding whether a twin is small for a twin. Using a twin-specific chart markedly reduces over-diagnosis of SGA/FGR without missing stillbirth or adverse outcomes.
    • Singleton-scale percentile — shows the magnitude of physiological twin growth divergence. In DC twins the median EFW sits near the singleton 22nd centile by 36 weeks; in MCDA twins near the 12th centile. This view is for context, not for an FGR diagnosis.
    • EFW discordance — (larger − smaller) ÷ larger × 100. Discordance > 25% is an independent risk factor for adverse perinatal outcome in both DC and MC twins; the SOGC suggests > 20% as a pragmatic, prudent threshold to prompt referral or input from a regional maternal–fetal medicine unit. In monochorionic twins, selective FGR is formally defined by a Delphi consensus (EFW of one twin < 3rd centile, or ≥ 2 of: EFW < 10th centile, AC < 10th centile, EFW discordance ≥ 25%, UA-PI of the smaller twin > 95th centile).
    • Discordance alone is not an indication for delivery — interpret alongside growth velocity, umbilical artery / MCA / ductus venosus Doppler, amniotic fluid, and gestational age.
    Reference & method

    The twin percentile curves are the chorionicity-specific charts from Wright et al. (FMF, 2024), derived from 4391 twin pregnancies (3323 DC, 1068 MCDA) across four European centres, with EFW calculated by the Hadlock HC + AC + FL formula. Percentiles are tabulated weekly (Tables 1 and 2 of the paper, 3rd / 10th / 50th / 90th / 97th centiles); this tool linearly interpolates between the tabulated weeks for intermediate gestational ages.

    The DC reference spans 20+3–37+3 weeks and the MCDA reference 16+3–37+3 weeks. EFW outside the chorionicity-specific window is plotted but not assigned a twin percentile. The singleton-scale view uses the FMF 2018 population weight chart.

    Limitations
    • EFW is locked to Hadlock-3 because that is the formula the FMF twin charts were built on; using a different EFW formula would not be consistent with the reference.
    • The FMF twin reference cohort delivered at ≥ 37 weeks (DC) or ≥ 36 weeks (MCDA) — i.e. uncomplicated pregnancies — so the curves describe normal twin growth, not the growth of complicated pregnancies.
    • Singleton-scale percentiles below ~20 weeks for MCDA twins rely on extrapolation of the singleton model and are less reliable; the twin-scale EFW percentile is not affected.
    • This tool does not classify selective FGR or assign a Gratacós type — those require umbilical artery Doppler. It does not adjust for fetal sex, ethnicity, or maternal characteristics.
    • Confirm dating (ideally from the larger first-trimester CRL or the ART conception date) before reading trajectories.
    Audit & reference
    EFW formula
    Hadlock-3 (HC + AC + FL), 1985 — locked
    Twin percentile source
    Wright A, Wright D, Chaveeva P, et al. Fetal Medicine Foundation charts for fetal growth in twins. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2024;63:181–188. Tables 1 (DC) & 2 (MCDA).
    Singleton percentile source
    Nicolaides KH, Wright D, Syngelaki A, et al. FMF fetal and neonatal population weight charts. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2018;52:44–51.
    Discordance thresholds
    > 25% independent adverse-outcome risk; > 20% pragmatic referral prompt (SOGC No. 428 / No. 440)
    GA range
    DC 20+3–37+3 wk · MCDA 16+3–37+3 wk
    Interpolation
    Linear between tabulated weekly centiles
    Version
    v0.1 (pending institutional review)
    REFERENCE · TEACHING PAGES

    How to scan

    Plane-by-plane technique guidance for first-trimester (11–14 wk) and routine mid-trimester (18–22 wk) fetal ultrasound. Each page covers the standard plane, structures to identify, caliper placement, and common pitfalls — drawn from ISUOG Practice Guidelines and the local McMaster MUMC protocol.

    First trimester · 11+0 – 14+0 weeks

    Combined screening protocol: confirm viability, establish gestational age, evaluate gross fetal anatomy, measure NT.

    Neck
    Nuchal translucency measurement
    Mid-sagittal section, neutral neck, calipers on-to-on. CRL window 45–84 mm.
    →
    Whole fetus
    First-trimester anatomy survey
    Complete structural checklist for the 11–14 wk scan: head, heart, abdomen, limbs, placenta.
    →
    Biometry
    Crown–rump length (CRL)
    Mid-sagittal, neutral neck, calipers on outer skin margins of crown and rump. CRL window 45–84 mm.
    →
    Face
    Nasal bone (first trimester)
    Three-line sign in mid-sagittal NT plane. Qualitative — present / absent / hypoplastic.
    →
    Doppler
    Ductus venosus & tricuspid flow
    DV: a-wave present vs absent / reversed. TR: holosystolic regurgitation ≥ 60 cm/s.
    →

    Mid-trimester anatomy · 18 – 22 weeks

    Complete plane-by-plane technique for the routine anatomy scan. Magnify the area of interest to fill ~75% of the image.

    Biometry

    Head
    HC & BPD measurement
    Transthalamic plane, CSP visible, calipers outer-to-outer (HC) and outer-to-inner (BPD).
    →
    Abdomen
    Abdominal circumference (AC)
    Coming next. Transverse, circular, stomach + umbilical vein at portal sinus, kidneys NOT visible.
    Soon
    Limb
    Femur length (FL)
    Coming next. Long-axis, both ends of ossified diaphysis visible, exclude triangular spurs.
    Soon

    Head, brain, face & neck

    Brain
    Brain — three axial planes
    Coming next. Transventricular · transthalamic · transcerebellar.
    Soon
    Face
    Face — profile, orbits, lips
    Coming next. Mid-sagittal profile, orbits coronal/axial, coronal nose-and-lips.
    Soon
    Neck
    Neck
    Coming next. Cylindrical, no protuberances or fluid collections.
    Soon

    Chest & heart

    Heart
    Four-chamber view (4CV)
    Axial, apex up, full rib bilaterally, AV-valve offset, moderator band, descending aorta posterior.
    →
    Heart
    Intraventricular septum (IVS)
    Color Doppler at 45–90° to septum, bidirectional ventricular filling, flow across foramen ovale.
    →
    Heart
    Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT)
    Slight rotation toward right shoulder + cephalad. Aorta from LV; medial wall continuous with IVS — rules out aortic override.
    →
    Heart
    Right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT)
    Further cephalad. Pulmonary trunk from RV directed posteriorly, branching into ductus + right pulmonary artery.
    →
    Heart
    Three-vessel view (3VV)
    Three vessels left-to-right: pulmonary arch · aortic arch · SVC. Approximately equal in size and aligned.
    →
    Heart
    3VT (three vessels & trachea)
    Highest transverse cardiac plane. Trachea right of aorta, behind SVC. V-shape arrowhead of aorta + ductus.
    →
    Thorax
    Diaphragm & lungs
    Coming next. Sagittal, intact diaphragm bilaterally, homogeneous lung fields.
    Soon

    Abdomen, spine, limbs

    Abdomen
    Abdominal situs & wall
    Coming next. Stomach left, IVC anterior & right of aorta, intact cord insertion.
    Soon
    Pelvis
    Bladder & umbilical arteries
    Coming next. Color Doppler trace of two UAs around the bladder.
    Soon
    Renal
    Kidneys
    Coming next. Axial bilateral, AP renal pelvis measurement, coronal if difficult.
    Soon
    Spine
    Spine — sagittal, axial, coronal
    Coming next. Intact skin line, three ossification centres forming equilateral triangle, sacrococcygeal tapering.
    Soon
    Upper limb
    Upper limbs
    Coming next. Humerus, radius/ulna, hands present, no persistent clenching.
    Soon
    Lower limb
    Lower limbs & feet
    Coming next. Femur, tibia/fibula equal length, square heel to rule out talipes.
    Soon

    Placenta, cord & cervix

    Placenta
    Placenta & cord insertion
    Coming next. Location relative to cervix, vasa-previa screen, PAS markers in scarred uterus.
    Soon
    Cervix
    Cervical length
    Coming next. Transabdominal screen; transvaginal if ≤ 30 mm or risk factors. Mid-sagittal, ± fundal pressure.
    Soon
    References cited across all pages: ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. UOG 2023;61:127–143 · ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of the routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. UOG 2022;59:840–856 · McMaster MUMC Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol (May 16, 2024) · Fetal Medicine Foundation NT-screening criteria (fetalmedicine.org).
    How to scan › First trimester › Nuchal translucency

    Nuchal translucency measurement

    Standardized FMF technique for NT measurement at 11+0–13+6 wk, CRL window 45–84 mm. The measurement is used as a likelihood ratio in combined first-trimester screening; accurate assessment is essential.

    Reference ISUOG 2023 · FMF UK · McMaster MUMC NT protocol v0.14
    Window & prerequisites
    • Gestation: 11+0 to 13+6 weeks.
    • CRL window: 45–84 mm (the measurement window for NT).
    • Approach: transabdominal first; transvaginal if maternal habitus, fibroids, or retroverted uterus limit transabdominal resolution.
    • Quality assurance: NT measurement should be performed only by personnel who have completed FMF (or equivalent) accreditation and who participate in continuous QA against an international standard.
    Plane & image criteria

    The NT measurement is taken in a true mid-sagittal section of the fetus. The image must satisfy all of the following:

    • Magnified to include only the fetal head and upper thorax — fetal image fills most of the screen.
    • Magnified such that each caliper movement produces a 0.1 mm change in the measurement.
    • Fetus in a neutral position — neither flexed (under-measures NT) nor hyperextended (over-measures NT).
    • Entire length of the nuchal region visualised; measurement at the maximum thickness.
    • Fetus clearly separate from the amnion — wait for spontaneous fetal movement, or have the woman cough, to differentiate.
    Caliper placement
    • Cross-calipers placed on the echogenic margins defining the NT thickness.
    • The crossbar of the caliper should be hardly visible — it should merge with the white line of the border ("on-to-on"), not be placed within the nuchal fluid.
    • Take at least three measurements on separate images. Record the maximum.

    Cord around the neck. If the umbilical cord encircles the neck, measure the NT both above and below the cord and use the average of the two for risk calculation.

    Cine-loop tip. Record a clip of the fetus moving away from the amniotic membrane to confirm the line being measured is fetal skin and not amnion.

    Common pitfalls
    • Over-measurement: hyperextended neck; calipers placed in the nuchal fluid rather than on the margins; mistaking the amnion for the posterior fetal skin.
    • Under-measurement: flexed neck; image not maximally magnified (each caliper step > 0.1 mm); measurement taken away from the maximum thickness.
    • Oblique section rather than true mid-sagittal — gives a falsely thin or thick measurement; recognise by inability to see the nasal bone, palate, and brainstem clearly in the same image.
    • Cord across the neck not recognised — leads to single-point over-measurement; always sweep with colour Doppler if NT appears unexpectedly thick.
    Reporting & downstream
    • Report the maximum NT in mm to one decimal, alongside the CRL.
    • Combine with maternal age, free β-hCG, PAPP-A (and PlGF if used) in the FMF mixture-model risk calculator.
    • Increased NT (≥ 95th centile, or absolute thresholds typically ≥ 3.5 mm) warrants offer of diagnostic testing, detailed early anatomy, and serial cardiac evaluation — increased NT marks up to 40% of major cardiac anomalies.
    NT in twin pregnancies

    The NT measurement technique is identical in twins and singletons — true mid-sagittal section, neutral neck, calipers on-to-on at the maximum thickness. What differs is interpretation and how risk is aggregated.

    • Per-fetus interpretation is unchanged: there is no separate NT normative chart for twins. Each fetus's NT is read against the standard CRL-based singleton centiles.
    • Inter-twin NT discordance — (larger − smaller) ÷ larger × 100 — is the twin-specific marker. NT discordance ≥ 20% is found in around a quarter of monochorionic twins, and in that group the risk of early intrauterine death or severe twin–twin transfusion syndrome exceeds 30%. ISUOG advises that twin pregnancies with NT discordance ≥ 20% (or CRL discordance ≥ 10%) be discussed with a fetal medicine expert. Predictive accuracy for TTTS is modest, so a normal result does not remove the need for biweekly surveillance of monochorionic twins from 16 weeks.
    • Aneuploidy risk aggregation: in monochorionic twins (shared karyotype) the trisomy-21 risk from combined first-trimester screening is calculated per pregnancy using the average NT; in dichorionic twins it is calculated per fetus.
    • Vanished twin: if a measurable fetal pole remains, serum free β-hCG and PAPP-A are unreliable — estimate risk from NT and maternal age alone.

    The Twin NT discordance calculator computes the discordance and flags the ≥ 20% threshold.

    References
    1. Bilardo CM, Chaoui R, Hyett JA, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2023;61:127–143.
    2. Nicolaides KH. Nuchal translucency and other first-trimester sonographic markers of chromosomal abnormalities. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2004;191:45–67.
    3. Fetal Medicine Foundation. The 11–13-week scan certification & QA program. fetalmedicine.org.
    4. Khalil A, Rodgers M, Baschat A, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: role of ultrasound in twin pregnancy. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2016;47:247–263.
    5. McMaster MUMC. Practice Guideline — Performance of the Nuchal Translucency Scan. Internal protocol, Dec 9 2025.
    How to scan › First trimester › Anatomy survey

    First-trimester anatomy survey

    Structured 11+0–14+0-week anatomical checklist. Performed alongside the NT measurement — combined first-trimester screening should never be limited to CRL and NT alone. Several severe structural anomalies can be detected in almost all cases at this gestation.

    Reference ISUOG 2023 · McMaster MUMC NT protocol v0.14
    Visualisation of many anatomical details by ultrasound is best achieved at around 13 weeks. Both transabdominal and transvaginal approaches may be required; a TV scan often gives better resolution in patients with high BMI, fibroids, or retroverted uterus.
    Recommended cine-loop documentation
    • One axial sweep through the fetus from head to pelvis.
    • One sagittal sweep through the fetus.
    • For multiple pregnancies: assess and document chorionicity (lambda sign / T-sign) and amnionicity.
    • Organs not well-seen at this scan can be assessed at the routine 18–22 wk anatomy scan; this should be documented.

    Checklist by anatomical region

    Biometry
    • Sagittal view: Crown–rump length (CRL) and nuchal translucency thickness.
    • Axial view: Biparietal diameter (BPD).
    • CRL is used to estimate gestational age in all cases except pregnancies conceived by IVF (use embryo transfer date). Use the best-quality CRL between 45 and 84 mm if multiple measurements have been taken.
    Head and brain

    Plane: axial view of head.

    • Calcification of cranium (cranial bone ossification by 11 completed weeks).
    • Contour and shape of cranium with no bony defects.
    • Two brain halves separated by interhemispheric falx.
    • Butterfly sign: choroid plexuses almost filling lateral ventricles in their posterior two-thirds.

    Optional best-practice (mid-sagittal): intracranial translucency (4th ventricle), brainstem, cisterna magna — useful to screen for open neural tube defects and posterior fossa malformations.

    Neck
    • Fetal head and upper thorax should occupy the entire screen.
    • Neutral position of the neck in a midsagittal view.
    • NT should be distinguished from amnion.
    • Measurement obtained in the widest portion.
    • Calipers placed "on-to-on".
    • Record cine-loop of fetus jumping away from the amniotic membrane (to confirm separation from amnion).
    • Document NT < 95th percentile for CRL.
    • No jugular cysts in the neck.
    Heart

    Plane: axial view of heart at the level of the four-chamber view.

    • Heart inside chest with regular rhythm.
    • Cardiac axis to the left (30°–60°).
    • Heart fills approximately one-third of thoracic space.
    • Two distinct ventricles on grayscale and color Doppler in diastole.
    • Axial sweep with and without colour Doppler.

    Best-practice additions: LVOT view, three-vessel view, three-vessels-and-trachea view, ductus venosus PIV, tricuspid regurgitation assessment.

    Abdomen

    Axial view:

    • Stomach visible in left upper abdomen (situs).
    • Intact abdominal wall — no umbilical defects. Note: physiologic midgut herniation is normal up to 11 weeks; differentiate from omphalocele/gastroschisis (the cord-insertion site is documented after 12 wk).
    • Two umbilical arteries bordering the bladder (paravesical color Doppler is helpful).
    • Kidneys: bilateral presence in paraspinal location.

    Axial or sagittal view:

    • Bladder visible and not dilated. Longitudinal bladder diameter < 7 mm at 12 wk.
    Extremities
    • Visualise four limbs, each with three segments.
    • Confirm normal orientation of both hands and both feet.
    • Open hands at this gestation are common and aid in assessing digits.
    Placenta & amniotic fluid
    • Placental appearance: slightly echogenic, uniform, homogeneous — no large or multiple cysts/lacunae.
    • Location in relation to cervix and to any previous Caesarean-section scar (early PAS-disorder screen if previous Caesarean and low anterior implantation).
    • Cord insertion into placenta documented.
    • Amniotic fluid volume: normal vs decreased/absent.
    • Amnion typically not yet fused with chorion at this gestation; this is physiological.
    Maternal anatomy
    • Uterus — fibroids, uterine anomalies (septum, bicornuate).
    • Adnexa (ovaries) — masses or cysts.
    • Cervix — length measurement may be added (transvaginal preferred for accurate CL).
    Pre-eclampsia screening (optional, integrated)

    If first-trimester combined screening for pre-eclampsia is offered, measure uterine artery PI at the time of NT assessment:

    • Sagittal section of the uterus, identify cervical canal and internal os.
    • Tilting the transducer gently to each side, with colour flow mapping, identify each UtA at the level of the internal os.
    • Pulsed-wave Doppler, sample gate 2 mm covering the whole vessel, insonation angle < 30°.
    • Three similar consecutive waveforms; UtA-PI by automatic tracing; calculate mean of left and right.
    • Combine with maternal factors, MAP, PlGF (and PAPP-A if available) in the FMF combined-test calculator.
    References
    1. Bilardo CM, Chaoui R, Hyett JA, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2023;61:127–143.
    2. Karim JN, Roberts NW, Salomon LJ, Papageorghiou AT. Systematic review of first-trimester ultrasound screening in detecting fetal structural anomalies and factors affecting screening performance. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2017;50:429–441.
    3. McMaster MUMC. Practice Guideline — Performance of the Nuchal Translucency Scan. Internal protocol, Dec 9 2025.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › HC & BPD

    Head circumference & biparietal diameter

    Standard mid-trimester biometry of the fetal head, taken in the transthalamic axial plane. The same plane is used for both HC and BPD; only the caliper technique differs.

    Reference ISUOG 2022 mid-trimester · ISUOG biometry 2019 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.14
    Magnify the head to fill ~75% of the image. Use the same caliper technique as the reference chart you score against — there is more than one valid technique and they are not interchangeable.
    Plane & structures to identify

    Plane: transverse axial view of the fetal head at the level of the thalami (transthalamic plane). Both HC and BPD are measured in this same plane.

    Quality criteria:

    • Ideal angle of insonation 90° to the midline echoes; minor variation acceptable.
    • Symmetrical appearance of both hemispheres.
    • Midline echo (falx cerebri) is interrupted only anteriorly by the cavum septi pellucidi.
    • Cerebellum not visible (if it is, the plane is too low — angle the probe more cephalad).

    Structures to demonstrate in the image:

    • Cavum septi pellucidi (CSP)
    • Thalami (paired hypoechoic, midline)
    • Hypoechoic skull bone interrupted by sutures
    • Third ventricle
    • Choroid plexus in lateral ventricles
    • Posterior horn of the lateral ventricle
    • Corpus callosum (anterior to CSP, hypoechoic structure)
    Caliper placement — BPD

    Two valid techniques exist; use the same one as your reference chart.

    • Outer-to-inner ("leading edge"): distal caliper on the outer edge of the proximal parietal bone, proximal caliper on the inner edge of the distal parietal bone.
    • Outer-to-outer: both calipers on the outer edges of the parietal bones. Recent ISUOG guidance prefers outer-to-outer for standardisation, reproducibility, and quality control.

    Place at the widest part of the skull, perpendicular to the midline.

    Cephalic index (BPD/OFD): a marker of head shape. Brachycephaly or dolichocephaly can falsely shift BPD-derived GA; in these cases, prefer HC for dating.

    Caliper placement — HC

    Two ways:

    • Direct ellipse: place the ellipse around the outside of the skull bone echoes (outer-to-outer). ISUOG-preferred for standardisation.
    • Calculated from BPD and OFD: HC = 1.62 × (BPD + OFD), where OFD is measured by placing calipers in the middle of the bone echo at frontal and occipital skull bones.
    Common pitfalls
    • Plane too low — cerebellum visible → angle cephalad. Falsely small BPD/HC.
    • Plane too high — CSP not visible, bilateral ventricles appear large → angle caudally.
    • Oblique section — falx not midline straight, hemispheres asymmetric → re-orient probe.
    • Caliper-technique mismatch with reference chart — silently shifts percentiles. Confirm chart specification before measuring.
    • Acoustic shadow from proximal parietal bone obscures the inner edge — re-angle, or use the contralateral skull as the proximal reference.
    • Head shape abnormality (oligohydramnios, breech, dolicho/brachycephaly) — HC remains reliable, BPD does not.
    Dating use

    If gestational age has not been established by an earlier high-quality scan, the HC alone, or HC plus FL, is the most accurate predictor of GA after 14 weeks (INTERGROWTH-21st). Subsequent scans should not re-date a pregnancy that has already been dated by a high-quality earlier scan.

    References
    1. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Berghella V, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of the routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2022;59:840–856.
    2. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Da Silva Costa F, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: ultrasound assessment of fetal biometry and growth. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2019;53:715–723.
    3. Napolitano R, Donadono V, Ohuma EO, et al. Scientific basis for standardization of fetal head measurements by ultrasound: a reproducibility study. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2016;48:80–85.
    4. Papageorghiou AT, Kemp B, Stones W, et al. Ultrasound-based gestational-age estimation in late pregnancy. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2016;48:719–726.
    5. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › Four-chamber view

    Four-chamber view (4CV)

    The cornerstone of mid-trimester cardiac screening. An axial view through the lower part of the fetal chest with apex superior, demonstrating both atria, both ventricles, the offset cross of AV valves, and the descending aorta posterior to the heart.

    Reference ISUOG 2022 mid-trimester · ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.14
    Single acoustic focal zone, narrow field of view to maximise frame rate. Magnify until the heart fills at least one-third to one-half of the screen.
    Acquisition
    • Plane: axial through the lower fetal chest, apex superior if achievable.
    • Frame: a full length of rib should be visible on each side — this confirms the section is truly axial and not oblique.
    • Magnification: heart occupies one-third to one-half of the displayed image.
    • Frame rate: single focal zone, narrow sector.
    Structures to identify
    • Right ventricle (RV) — closer to the anterior chest wall, contains the moderator band at the apex.
    • Left ventricle (LV) — extends to the apex of the heart.
    • Right atrium (RA).
    • Left atrium (LA) — pulmonary veins entering it (look for at least one).
    • Foramen ovale with leaflet visible, moving in the LA, no more than halfway into the atrial cavity.
    • Crux of the heart demonstrating offset arrangement of AV valves: tricuspid (right) inserts closer to the apex than the mitral (left).
    • Descending aorta — single vessel posterior to the heart, just left of the spinal ossification centre.
    • Ribs bilaterally, full-length.
    • Pericardial space — a thin rim (≤ 2 mm) of physiological fluid is acceptable.
    Quality checklist
    • Size: heart ≤ 1/3 of thoracic area (no cardiomegaly).
    • Axis: 45 ± 20° to the left (60% of fetuses 30°–60°).
    • Symmetry: RV and LV approximately equal in size and width at the AV-valve level; both extend to the apex.
    • Rate & rhythm: regular, 120–160 bpm. Document with M-mode or pulsed-wave Doppler.
    • Situs: heart and stomach both on the left (with documentation of fetal lie — concordant L/L is needed but not sufficient for situs solitus).
    • AV valves: normal opening/closing, no thickening, no focal echogenicity.
    • Septum: intact ventricular septum on grayscale; perpendicular insonation for colour Doppler check (45°–90° to septum).
    • Color flow: bidirectional flow into both ventricles all the way to the apex; flow across the foramen ovale.
    Sweep technique

    The 4CV is the start of an axial sweep from the stomach to the 3VV (or vice versa), with and without colour Doppler:

    1. Stomach (left abdominal) — confirms abdominal situs.
    2. 4CV — described above.
    3. LVOT — slight rotation toward the right shoulder, slight cephalad tilt; aorta arising from LV, IVS continuous with medial aortic wall (rules out aortic override).
    4. RVOT — further cephalad; pulmonary trunk arising from RV, directed posteriorly toward spine, branching into ductus and right pulmonary artery.
    5. 3VV — three vessels left-to-right: pulmonary arch, aortic arch, SVC.
    6. 3VT — same level + trachea (echogenic walls, right of aorta, behind SVC); V-shape "arrowhead" of aorta and ductus meeting.
    Common pitfalls
    • Apex-down view — workable but flow direction in colour Doppler reversed; offset of AV valves harder to appreciate.
    • Oblique section (rib visible on only one side) — falsely asymmetric chambers, misleading axis. Re-orient until full rib visible bilaterally.
    • Spine anterior (fetal supine position) — rib shadows obscure the heart; wait for fetal movement, or re-scan from a different maternal angle.
    • Acoustic shadow over far-field structures — use the contralateral chamber for symmetry assessment, and confirm with sweep.
    • Persistent left SVC can mimic enlarged coronary sinus on the 4CV — drop down a single plane to confirm.
    • Apparent ventricular asymmetry — small differences (RV slightly > LV) are normal; persistent disproportion warrants formal fetal echocardiography (consider coarctation).
    • Echogenic intracardiac focus (EIF) — a discrete bright spot in the LV, common (3–5%); isolated EIF without other markers is a "soft" marker only.
    References
    1. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Berghella V, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of the routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2022;59:840–856.
    2. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    3. Comstock CH. Normal fetal heart axis and position. Obstet Gynecol 1987;70:255–259.
    4. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › Intraventricular septum

    Intraventricular septum (IVS)

    Color-Doppler interrogation of the four-chamber view to confirm an intact intraventricular septum, with bidirectional ventricular filling all the way to the apex and forward flow across the foramen ovale.

    Reference ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.16
    Color flow should reach both ventricular apices. Asymmetric or unidirectional filling, or any high-velocity jet through the septum, mandates referral for fetal echocardiography.
    Plane & insonation angle

    Starting view: the standard four-chamber view (apex up if achievable, full rib visible bilaterally).

    • Insonation angle: the septum should sit between 45° and 90° (towards horizontal) to the ultrasound beam. This greatly improves Doppler sensitivity to flow across (and through) the septum.
    • If the heart is apex-up with the septum vertical (parallel to the beam), angle the probe to bring the septum more horizontal before applying colour.
    • Single focal zone, narrow colour box covering both ventricles.
    What you should see in diastole
    • Bidirectional ventricular filling: color flow into both the right and left ventricles, all the way to the apex.
    • Foramen ovale: right-to-left flow visible across the foramen.
    • Septum integrity: no colour bleeding across the muscular or membranous septum.
    • Symmetry: the depth of colour penetration into each ventricle should be roughly symmetrical.
    Common pitfalls
    • Septum parallel to beam (apex-up, septum vertical) — colour Doppler is insensitive at this angle and ventricular septal defects can be missed. Always re-orient.
    • Excessive colour gain creates blooming artefact and false bidirectional flow at the septum. Reduce gain to threshold.
    • Pulse repetition frequency (PRF) too high for the low diastolic filling velocities → no colour fills the ventricle. Reduce PRF/scale.
    • Doppler artefacts at the moderator band (RV) can mimic flow disruption. Confirm with a different angle.
    • Asymmetric filling with the LV smaller than the RV — consider hypoplastic left heart, coarctation, aortic stenosis. Refer.
    References
    1. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    2. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Berghella V, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of the routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2022;59:840–856.
    3. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › LVOT

    Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT)

    From the four-chamber view, slight rotation toward the right shoulder and small cephalad tilt brings the aorta into view as it arises from the left ventricle. The medial aortic wall must be continuous with the interventricular septum — this rules out aortic override (tetralogy of Fallot, double-outlet RV, truncus).

    Reference ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.16
    Routine assessment of both outflow tracts in addition to the 4CV materially increases detection of conotruncal anomalies (TOF, TGA, DORV, truncus arteriosus communis).
    Acquisition
    1. Start at the four-chamber view.
    2. Rotate the probe slightly toward the fetal right shoulder.
    3. Move the probe slightly cephalad while maintaining the axial section.
    4. The aorta appears arising from the LV, directed toward the right shoulder; at a higher level it turns back toward the left to form the aortic arch.
    5. The aortic valve cusps can often be seen opening and closing in the centre of the vessel.
    Structures to identify
    • Right ventricle
    • Left ventricle
    • Rib (full length to confirm axial plane)
    • Spine
    • Descending aorta (posterior, paraspinal)
    • Left atrium — tip of left atrial appendage may be visible
    • Right atrium
    • Ascending aorta arising from the LV, with the aortic valve at its base — the medial wall continuous with the interventricular septum
    Color Doppler assessment
    • Forward flow across the aortic valve.
    • No turbulence (no aliasing or mosaic pattern) at the level of the valve or in the ascending aorta.
    • Flow direction: from LV into ascending aorta, away from the heart toward the right shoulder.
    Common pitfalls
    • Plane too cephalad — you skip past LVOT and land in RVOT. Reverse a small step.
    • Septoaortic continuity not assessed — must explicitly confirm the medial aortic wall continues directly from the IVS without any defect or override.
    • Turbulence in ascending aorta — consider valvular aortic stenosis. Refer for fetal echo.
    • Small or absent ascending aorta — hypoplastic left heart spectrum.
    • Aorta arising from RV (or both ventricles) — TGA / DORV. Can be subtle on the LVOT view alone; the 3VT view confirms abnormal arch arrangement.
    References
    1. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    2. Salomon LJ et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. UOG 2022;59:840–856.
    3. Everwijn SMP, van Nisselrooij AEL, Rozendaal L, et al. The effect of the introduction of the three-vessel view on the detection rate of transposition of the great arteries and tetralogy of Fallot. Prenat Diagn 2018;38:951–957.
    4. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › RVOT

    Right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT)

    Still axial, move further cephalad from the LVOT view. The pulmonary trunk arises from the RV close to the sternum, directed posteriorly toward the spine, and bifurcates into the ductus arteriosus (continuing toward the spine) and the right pulmonary artery (curving behind the ascending aorta and SVC).

    Reference ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.16
    Bifurcation of the pulmonary trunk into ductus arteriosus and right pulmonary artery is the key sign that the vessel arising from the RV is going to the lungs (as opposed to TGA). The crossing pattern of the two great vessels is what distinguishes them on screening — they should not run parallel.
    Acquisition
    1. Starting from the LVOT view, move the probe further cephalad, maintaining axial.
    2. The pulmonary trunk emerges from the RV, anterior and close to the sternum.
    3. The trunk is directed posteriorly toward the spine, then bifurcates into:
      • Ductus arteriosus — continues directly toward the spine, joining the descending aorta in the midline.
      • Right pulmonary artery (RPA) — curves behind the ascending aorta and SVC.
    4. The left pulmonary artery is not usually visible in this plane — it is directed more inferiorly.
    Structures to identify
    • Right ventricle
    • Pulmonary valve
    • Pulmonary trunk
    • Ductus arteriosus
    • Descending aorta (in continuity with the ductus at the midline)
    • Right pulmonary artery (curving behind the great vessels)
    • Superior vena cava
    • Ascending aorta
    • Right atrium
    Color Doppler assessment
    • No turbulence across the pulmonary valve.
    • Direction of flow down the ductus arteriosus is correct — from the pulmonary trunk toward the descending aorta.
    • The flow pattern in the pulmonary trunk and ductus is unidirectional and laminar.
    Common pitfalls
    • Parallel great vessels (the two outflows running side by side rather than crossing) — TGA. Confirm with the 3VT view.
    • Pulmonary trunk smaller than aorta — pulmonary stenosis or atresia. Refer.
    • Reversed flow in the ductus — severe pulmonary stenosis/atresia or severe right-sided obstruction.
    • Turbulence at pulmonary valve — pulmonary stenosis.
    • Confusing RPA for the descending aorta — track the ductus to the midline; the RPA does not reach the spine.
    References
    1. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    2. Salomon LJ et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. UOG 2022;59:840–856.
    3. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › 3VV

    Three-vessel view (3VV)

    A further cephalad axial section from the RVOT view. From left to right: top of the pulmonary arch · aortic arch · superior vena cava. The three vessels should be approximately equal in size and aligned in a near-straight line.

    Reference ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.16
    Adding the 3VV/3VT views to routine cardiac screening substantially improves detection of great-vessel anomalies, aortic arch lesions, and systemic vein anomalies beyond what the four-chamber view alone can show.
    Acquisition
    1. From the RVOT view, move the transducer further cephalad, maintaining axial.
    2. You arrive at a plane where three discrete circular/oval cross-sections of vessels are visible.
    3. From left to right of the screen (with fetal left to image left in apex-up orientation):
      • Top of the pulmonary arch (largest, leftmost)
      • Aortic arch
      • Superior vena cava (smallest, rightmost)
    What you should see
    • Three vessels in a row, near-straight alignment from left to right.
    • Size relationship: pulmonary arch ≥ aortic arch > SVC. The pulmonary arch (sometimes labelled "Pa" or "Da" depending on convention) is slightly larger than the aorta in normal fetuses.
    • Position: all three vessels are anterior to the spine; the aorta is just to the left of the spinal ossification centres.
    Common pitfalls & abnormal patterns
    • Two vessels only — consider truncus arteriosus, pulmonary atresia with single arterial trunk.
    • Parallel pulmonary trunk and aorta with abnormal alignment — TGA.
    • Pulmonary arch smaller than aorta — pulmonary stenosis / TOF.
    • Aorta smaller than pulmonary arch (markedly) — coarctation, hypoplastic aortic arch.
    • Right-sided aortic arch — abnormal vessel arrangement on the 3VV; often part of conotruncal-anomaly spectrum (TOF, 22q11 deletion).
    • Persistent left SVC — additional vessel to the left of the pulmonary arch (4 vessels in cross-section instead of 3).
    References
    1. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    2. Yoo SJ, Lee YH, Kim ES, et al. Three-vessel view of the fetal upper mediastinum. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 1997;9:173–182.
    3. Anton T, Sklansky MS, Perez M, Pretorius DH. The fetal 3-vessel views: an illustrative case-based tutorial. J Ultrasound Med 2019;38:3335–3347.
    4. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › Mid-trimester › 3VT

    Three-vessels-and-trachea view (3VT)

    The highest transverse plane for evaluating the fetal heart. Both arches converge as a "V"-shaped arrowhead just left of the spine; the trachea sits to the right of the aorta and behind the SVC. Width of the ductus is slightly greater than that of the aorta.

    Reference ISUOG cardiac screening 2013 · McMaster MUMC protocol v0.16
    An aberrant right subclavian artery (ARSA) — passing behind the trachea toward the right shoulder — is seen in ~1% of fetuses and is associated with an increased risk of trisomy 21. Look for it routinely on this plane (especially with colour Doppler).
    Acquisition
    1. From the RVOT view, move the transducer further cephalad, maintaining the axial section.
    2. The 3VT plane is reached just above the 3VV.
    3. Apply colour Doppler over the arches to confirm flow direction and the V-shape arrowhead.
    Structures to identify
    • Pulmonary arch (ductus arteriosus joining the descending aorta) — leftmost.
    • Aortic arch — meets the ductus to form the V-shape arrowhead just left of the spine; now directed toward the left shoulder.
    • Superior vena cava (SVC) — rightmost, smallest of the three.
    • Trachea — to the right of the aorta and behind the SVC; echogenic walls distinguish it from the surrounding vessels.
    • Spine (posterior, paraspinal descending aorta).
    • Rib (full length confirms the axial plane).
    Quality criteria
    • V-shape arrowhead: the aortic and ductal arches must meet to form a single confluence just left of the spine.
    • Vessel sizes: ductus slightly > aorta > SVC.
    • Vessel positions: all anterior to the trachea; trachea to the right of the aorta.
    • Colour Doppler: both arches show flow away from the heart (correct direction). Mosaic / reversed flow is abnormal.

    Demonstration of the aortic and ductal arches as separate sagittal long-axis views, plus SVC and IVC, is not routine in screening anatomy and is reserved for fetal echo.

    Common pitfalls & abnormal patterns
    • U-sign (instead of V-sign) — right aortic arch with left ductus surrounding the trachea. Up to ~30% association with 22q11 deletion; refer.
    • No V-shape, parallel arches — TGA, double-aortic arch.
    • Aortic arch markedly smaller than pulmonary arch — coarctation, hypoplastic aortic arch.
    • Aberrant right subclavian artery (ARSA) — vessel posterior to the trachea heading toward the right shoulder. Soft marker for trisomy 21; document but isolated finding has low LR+.
    • Reversed flow in either arch on colour Doppler — severe ipsilateral obstruction.
    • Persistent left SVC — additional vessel to the left of the pulmonary arch; often associated with coronary sinus dilatation on the 4CV.
    References
    1. Carvalho JS, Allan LD, Chaoui R, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): sonographic screening examination of the fetal heart. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:348–359.
    2. Yagel S, Arbel R, Anteby EY, Raveh D, Achiron R. The three vessels and trachea view (3VT) in fetal cardiac scanning. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2002;20:340–345.
    3. Bravo C, Gámez F, Pérez R, et al. Fetal Aortic Arch Anomalies: Key Sonographic Views for Their Differential Diagnosis and Clinical Implications Using the Cardiovascular System Sonographic Evaluation Protocol. J Ultrasound Med 2016;35:237–251.
    4. McMaster MUMC. Performance of the Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol. May 16, 2024.
    How to scan › First trimester › Crown–rump length

    Crown–rump length (CRL) measurement

    The reference biometry for first-trimester gestational-age dating and the gateway measurement for combined screening (NT, nasal bone, DV, TR). Strict mid-sagittal plane, neutral neck, calipers placed at the outer skin margins of crown and rump.

    Reference ISUOG 2023 11–14 wk · Robinson 1973 · McMaster MUMC FT protocol v0.18 · 11–14 wk
    Window & prerequisites
    • Gestation: CRL is the dating measurement of choice from ≈ 7 wk through 13+6 wk; the screening-eligible window is CRL 45–84 mm (≈ 11+0–13+6 wk).
    • Approach: transabdominal first; transvaginal if maternal habitus, fibroids, or retroversion limits resolution.
    • Magnification: the fetus should fill at least three-quarters of the displayed image, with caliper steps no coarser than 0.1 mm.
    • Movement: wait for the fetus to assume a neutral, motionless position. Do not measure during active movement, flexion, or hyperextension.
    Plane & image criteria

    A correct CRL image is a true mid-sagittal section in which all of the following are simultaneously visible:

    • Echogenic tip of the nose, palate, and the rectangular shape of the diencephalon (third ventricle echo).
    • The whole length of the fetus, from the apex of the head (crown) to the most caudal point of the trunk/rump.
    • Spine in profile, without rotation, so that vertebral echoes form a single line rather than paired echoes.
    • Neutral neck — no flexion (which under-measures) and no hyperextension (which over-measures and includes neck soft tissue).
    • Amniotic fluid clearly between the fetal chin/sternum and the rump.

    Caliper placement: calipers at the outer skin margins of the cranial vertex and the caudal rump. Do not include the yolk sac, umbilical cord, or a flexed lower limb in the measurement.

    Common pitfalls
    • Hyperextension: appears as an artificially long fetus with the chin lifted; causes over-measurement and falsely advanced GA. Wait for movement and re-image.
    • Flexion: chin tucked onto chest; causes under-measurement and falsely earlier GA.
    • Oblique section: paired vertebral echoes, foreshortened limbs, or both orbits visible — section is not truly mid-sagittal. Re-acquire.
    • Yolk sac in the measurement: the yolk sac sits adjacent to the abdomen and is sometimes mistaken for fetal trunk; it should be excluded.
    • Wrong end-points: caliper on the chorion / amnion rather than on the fetus, or caliper placed on a flexed lower limb.
    • Sub-optimal magnification: when the fetus fills less than 3/4 of the image, each caliper step is too coarse and rounding error inflates uncertainty.
    Reporting & downstream
    • Record CRL in mm to one decimal. Multiple acquisitions are encouraged; report the largest valid measurement.
    • If multiple dating modalities exist, the first high-quality first-trimester scan sets the EDD for the rest of the pregnancy. Do not re-date on later scans except in narrowly defined circumstances.
    • The CRL also defines the eligibility window for the rest of the combined-screening package (NT, NB, DV, TR) and for early anatomy.
    • If CRL is < 45 mm: too early for combined screening — re-scan in 1–2 weeks. If > 84 mm: window has closed; offer second-trimester serum screening or cfDNA per local pathway.

    The Compass CRL dating calculator reports GA and EDD across three published nomograms (Robinson 1973, Hadlock 1992, Daya 1993) for inter-reference cross-check.

    References
    1. Bilardo CM, Chaoui R, Hyett JA, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2023;61:127–143.
    2. Robinson HP, Fleming JE. A critical evaluation of sonar crown-rump length measurements. Br J Obstet Gynaecol 1975;82:702–710.
    3. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Bilardo CM, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: performance of first-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:102–113.
    4. McMaster MUMC. Practice Guidelines — Performance of the First-Trimester Fetal US Scan. Internal protocol.
    How to scan › First trimester › Nasal bone

    Nasal bone — first-trimester assessment

    Qualitative assessment of nasal-bone echogenicity in the same mid-sagittal plane used for NT. Absence of the nasal bone at 11+0–13+6 wk is a recognised marker for trisomy 21 and contributes to the combined-screening risk in the FMF mixture model.

    Reference ISUOG 2023 11–14 wk · Cicero 2001 · Sonek 2006 · McMaster MUMC FT protocol v0.18 · 11–14 wk
    Window & prerequisites
    • Gestation: 11+0–13+6 wk; CRL 45–84 mm. Same window as NT and combined screening.
    • Quality assurance: performed only by personnel with FMF (or equivalent) accreditation, with continuous QA against an international standard.
    • Plane: the mid-sagittal NT plane; nasal-bone status should be documented from the same image series.
    Plane & image criteria

    For a valid FT nasal-bone assessment, the image must demonstrate the three-line sign in true mid-sagittal section:

    1. Top line — overlying nasal skin: echogenic, continuous.
    2. Middle line — nasal bone: echogenic, equal to or thicker than the skin, lying just deep to it and parallel.
    3. Bottom line — nasal tip: echogenic stipple anterior to the bone.
    • Insonation angle 45° (oblique) to the long axis of the fetal nose, or perpendicular for documentation.
    • Image magnified so that head and upper thorax fill ≥ 75% of the screen — same magnification standard as NT.
    • Fetal face up (towards the transducer) is preferred but not mandatory.
    Categorisation

    First-trimester NB assessment is qualitative (present vs absent / hypoplastic), not measured as a continuous variable at this gestation:

    • Present (normal): middle line clearly visible, echogenicity equal to or greater than the skin line.
    • Absent: only two lines visible (skin + tip); the middle line is missing.
    • Hypoplastic: middle line present but distinctly less echogenic than the skin (sometimes called the "thin" or "faint" sign). Treat as abnormal in FMF combined-screening pathways.

    Quantitative NB length percentile is a mid-trimester assessment with multiple available references — see the Compass Nasal bone calculator (Sonek 2003, Bunduki 2003, Odibo 2007 MoM, Cicero 2003, Bromley 2002 BPD/NBL ratio).

    Common pitfalls
    • Oblique plane: only one or two of the three lines are visible; either the skin merges with bone (under-detection of absence) or the bone is artefactually missing (over-detection). Re-acquire a true mid-sagittal section.
    • Suboptimal angle of insonation: angle < 45° to the long axis of the nose can attenuate the bone echo and mimic absence. Adjust probe angulation.
    • Mistaking the nasal tip for the bone: the tip is always present and echogenic; if the operator labels the tip as the bone, true absence will be missed.
    • Fetal facial position: with the face down, both the bone and the skin can be obscured — wait for the fetus to turn or apply gentle abdominal manoeuvres.
    • Ethnic variation: NB absence rates differ between ancestral groups in euploid fetuses (higher in Afro-Caribbean cohorts); use ethnicity-specific likelihood ratios where available.
    Reporting & downstream
    • Document NB status as present, absent, or hypoplastic on the FT-screening report alongside CRL, NT, and (if performed) DV/TR.
    • Absent or hypoplastic NB combined with increased NT increases the LR for trisomy 21 markedly; integrate via the FMF mixture model rather than apply LRs in series ad hoc.
    • Even with a low overall risk, isolated absent NB in the absence of cfDNA results should prompt offer of expanded counselling.
    References
    1. Bilardo CM, Chaoui R, Hyett JA, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2023;61:127–143.
    2. Cicero S, Curcio P, Papageorghiou A, Sonek J, Nicolaides K. Absence of nasal bone in fetuses with trisomy 21 at 11–14 weeks of gestation. Lancet 2001;358:1665–1667.
    3. Sonek JD, Cicero S, Neiger R, Nicolaides KH. Nasal bone assessment in prenatal screening for trisomy 21. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2006;195:1219–1230.
    4. Fetal Medicine Foundation. The 11–13-week scan certification & QA program. fetalmedicine.org.
    5. McMaster MUMC. Practice Guidelines — Performance of the First-Trimester Fetal US Scan. Internal protocol.
    How to scan › First trimester › Ductus venosus & tricuspid Doppler

    Ductus venosus & tricuspid valve Doppler (11–14 wk)

    Two contingent markers in combined first-trimester screening for trisomies and major cardiac defects. Both are qualitative at this gestation: the DV is read as a-wave present versus absent or reversed; the tricuspid valve is read as TR present versus absent. Performance hinges on rigorous insonation technique.

    Reference ISUOG 2023 11–14 wk · Maiz 2009 · Falcon 2006 · Huggon 2003 v0.18 · 11–14 wk
    Window & prerequisites
    • Gestation: 11+0–13+6 wk; CRL 45–84 mm. DV and TR are assessed during the same examination as CRL, NT, and nasal bone.
    • Indication: contingent markers — most commonly used to refine intermediate-risk results from the FMF combined screen, or as components of the full mixture model.
    • Quality assurance: FMF (or equivalent) accreditation for both DV and TR is required; performance and false-positive rates depend critically on technique adherence.
    • Acoustic output: spectral Doppler in the first trimester carries higher TI and MI; use the lowest power consistent with diagnostic information and limit insonation time (ALARA).
    DV — plane & image criteria
    • Plane: mid-sagittal or right oblique parasagittal section of the fetal trunk; the DV is identified at colour Doppler as the high-velocity narrow channel between the umbilical vein and the inferior vena cava.
    • Sample volume: 0.5–1.0 mm, placed at the isthmic (inlet) portion of the DV — the narrowed entry from the umbilical vein.
    • Insonation angle: < 30° to flow direction.
    • Sweep speed: high (2–3 cm/s) so that one or two complete cardiac cycles fill the screen.
    • Filter: wall-motion filter as low as possible (50–70 Hz) so the a-wave is not artefactually clipped to baseline.
    • Quiescence: measure during a period of fetal rest (no breathing or trunk movement).
    DV — interpretation
    • Normal: triphasic forward flow throughout the cardiac cycle. Three peaks visible — S (ventricular systole), D (passive ventricular filling), a (atrial contraction). The a-wave is the lowest velocity but remains above the baseline.
    • Abnormal: a-wave at or below baseline (absent or reversed). Reflects increased atrial pressure during atrial contraction — found in fetal cardiac defects, trisomies, and a fraction of euploid fetuses.
    • Quantitative DV-PIV is reported on combined-screening platforms; FMF software converts to a delta-MoM. The Compass build does not currently include a DV-PIV percentile calculator (see roadmap).
    TR — plane & image criteria
    • Plane: apical four-chamber view of the fetal heart, with the apex pointing towards the transducer.
    • Sample volume: 2.0–3.0 mm, placed across the tricuspid valve to capture both inflow and any regurgitant jet.
    • Mode: pulsed-wave Doppler; continuous-wave is acceptable if available.
    • Insonation angle: < 30° to the inter-ventricular septum (and therefore to the regurgitant jet, which is approximately parallel to the septum).
    • Sweep speed: high; record at least three consecutive cardiac cycles.
    • Repeat: sample the valve at three different points along its closure line — TR may be eccentric and present at only one segment.
    TR — interpretation
    • Normal: diastolic E and A inflow above baseline; nothing below baseline during systole.
    • Abnormal: a regurgitant jet ≥ 60 cm/s below baseline that lasts > ½ of systole. Brief low-velocity systolic flicker is a normal finding and does not count as TR.
    • TR markedly increases the LR for trisomy 21 in the FMF mixture model and is also associated with major cardiac defects in euploid fetuses — abnormal TR mandates a detailed early cardiac evaluation.
    Common pitfalls
    • DV sample misplaced: placing the gate too far downstream (in the IVC) loses the characteristic high-velocity DV signal and can falsely show an apparently absent a-wave.
    • Wall-filter set too high: legitimately positive but low-velocity a-waves are clipped to baseline, mimicking absent / reversed flow.
    • Fetal breathing / movement: any motion will distort the waveform — measure during quiescence.
    • Mistaking pulmonary vein flow for DV: especially when the trunk plane is too cranial; confirm with colour Doppler that the sampled vessel passes from the umbilical recess to the IVC inlet.
    • TR — single sample line: regurgitation can be eccentric. A negative single-line sample does not exclude TR; sweep three points across the valve.
    • Wide sample volume across whole heart: contaminates with adjacent inflow / outflow and creates spectral noise that can be over-called as TR.
    • Brief closure-velocity click: a normal low-velocity, short-duration signal at valve closure is not TR. Holosystolic duration and ≥ 60 cm/s are both required.
    Reporting & downstream
    • Report DV as a-wave present (normal) or a-wave absent / reversed (abnormal), alongside the DV-PIV value if entered into FMF software.
    • Report TR as present or absent, with notation of holosystolic duration and peak velocity.
    • Either marker abnormal increases the trisomy 21 LR (≈ 3–11×) and prompts diagnostic-testing offer plus detailed early cardiac evaluation, even when the integrated risk remains modest.
    • Both markers normal in the setting of an abnormal NT or biochemistry will reduce but not eliminate the integrated risk.
    References
    1. Bilardo CM, Chaoui R, Hyett JA, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines (updated): performance of 11–14-week ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2023;61:127–143.
    2. Maiz N, Valencia C, Kagan KO, Wright D, Nicolaides KH. Ductus venosus Doppler in screening for trisomies 21, 18 and 13 and Turner syndrome at 11–13 weeks of gestation. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2009;33:512–517.
    3. Falcon O, Faiola S, Huggon I, Allan L, Nicolaides KH. Fetal tricuspid regurgitation at the 11+0 to 13+6-week scan: association with chromosomal defects and reproducibility of the method. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2006;27:609–612.
    4. Huggon IC, DeFigueiredo DB, Allan LD. Tricuspid regurgitation in the diagnosis of chromosomal anomalies in the fetus at 11–14 weeks of gestation. Heart 2003;89:1071–1073.
    5. Salomon LJ, Alfirevic Z, Bilardo CM, et al. ISUOG Practice Guidelines: performance of first-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. Ultrasound Obstet Gynecol 2013;41:102–113.

    Coming next

    This scan-guide page is on the build queue. Content will be drawn from the ISUOG Practice Guidelines and the McMaster MUMC mid-trimester protocol with full plane / structures / caliper / pitfalls coverage.

    Pending ISUOG 2022 · McMaster MUMC protocol

    In the meantime, see the How to scan landing page for the full set, and consult the source documents:

    • ISUOG Practice Guidelines: routine mid-trimester fetal ultrasound scan. UOG 2022;59:840–856.
    • ISUOG Practice Guidelines: 11–14-week ultrasound scan. UOG 2023;61:127–143.
    • McMaster MUMC Routine Mid-trimester Fetal Ultrasound Scan Protocol (May 16, 2024).

    Important — clinical use Compass is intended as clinical decision support and education for qualified clinicians. It does not replace clinical judgement, local protocols, or consultation with the appropriate specialist. Results must be interpreted in the full clinical context. The tool has not been certified as a medical device under Health Canada's SaMD framework. No patient identifiers are collected, stored, or transmitted. Users should verify the cited source and confirm applicability before acting on any output.
